studyMetadata.description1: This first volume invites pastors’ wives to lay down the weight of performance and return to the bedrock of who they are in Christ. Beginning with the foundational question of identity — not what you do, but who you are — it moves through the intimacy of walking with Jesus, the beauty and complexity of marriage as partnership, and the hidden interior life that God sees and tends. It does not shy away from the hard terrain: the loneliness, the criticism, the moments of failure that ministry life so often brings. And it closes not with a call to do more, but with an invitation to rest — to discover that sustainability is not laziness but wisdom, and that a woman who knows her own name before God is far better equipped to serve, love, and lead than one who has lost herself in her role.
studyMetadata.description2: Wannan juzu’i na farko yana kiran matan fastoci da su ajiye nauyin nuna bajinta su koma ga asalin wacece su a cikin Almasihu. An fara ne da tambayar asali game da wayewar kai — ba abin da kuke yi ba, amma wacece ku — sannan ya tafi ga kusanci na tafiya tare da Yesu, kyawu da sarƙaƙiyar aure a matsayin abokan tarayya, da kuma rayuwar ciki ɓoyayye wacce Allah yake gani kuma yake kula da ita. Ba ya tsoron magana kan mawuyacin yanayi: kadaici, suka, da lokutan gazawa waɗanda rayuwar hidima take yawan kawowa. Kuma ba a rufe shi da kiran a ƙara yin aiki ba, amma da gayyata zuwa ga hutu — don gano cewa dawwama ba rago ba ne amma hikima ce, kuma macen da ta san sunanta na kansa a gaban Allah tana da cikakken shiri don yin hidima, ƙauna, da jagoranci fiye da wadda ta ɓata kanta a cikin matsayinta.
studyMetadata.description3: Ngal juzu’i arangal ɗon nodda rewɓe pastor’en ngam ɓe njippina teddeendi kuugal ngal ɓe ngadata, ɓe koota to asalu mo ɓe ngoni nder Almasiihu. Ɗum ɗon fuɗɗira bee ƴamol asalu dow annditugol hoore — naa ɗume a waɗata, amma moye a woni — nden ɗum yaha to ɓadtotooɗal yahdu bee Yeesu, booɗɗum e caɗeele teegal bana kuuɗe kawtal, e ngendam nder yeerteru ɗam Allah yi’ata e kakkilanta. Walaa hultungo wolwugo dow nokkuuje caɗtuɗe: kankoore, aybinande, e sa’aaji ronkere ɗi ngendam kuugal Allah ɓuri addugo. Ɗum hadataa bee noddaandu ngam waɗugo kuuɗe feere, amma bee noddaandu to siwtare — ngam anndugo wondugo dow laawol naa hulyere on, amma hikma on, nden debbo annduɗo innde mum e hoore mum yeeso Allah ɓuri heboyugo baawɗe ngam waɗugo kuugal, yiide, e ardaaki dow debbo majjuɗo hoore mum nder daraja mum.

theme.theme-annotation: Terracotta study theme — warm ochre, burnt sienna, and hillside green palette. Grounded and vibrant, evoking African earth tones, harvest, and communal labour. Suited to studies on identity, calling, womanhood, or vocation. Warm and energising rather than restful.

elements.text1:
They had been at the same denominational women’s conference, sitting at the same table, and within twenty minutes Yetunde knew they had almost nothing in common — except the role.

Across from her was Sister Grace: dignified, softly spoken, impeccably dressed, present at every church function and never the centre of it. She taught the women’s Sunday school class, visited the sick on Wednesday afternoons, and was widely regarded as the ideal pastor’s wife. When asked about her gifts, she said simply: “I support my husband.” She said it without resentment or embarrassment. It was the truest thing she knew about herself.

Beside Yetunde was Pastor Ngozi’s wife — Chiamaka — who ran a legal aid clinic for women out of the church compound three days a week, preached in the women’s service twice a month, led a Bible study for market women on Friday mornings, and had, the previous year, started a network connecting church planters’ wives across three states. She was expansive, opinionated, and clearly uncomfortable sitting still for very long.

Both women loved Jesus. Both had faithful marriages. Both were serving their churches and their communities. And yet they could not have been more different.

Who was right? Who was the real pastor’s wife?

The answer the Bible gives is: both of them. And neither of them has the right to insist the other one should look more like herself.
Part 1 — Many Gifts, One Spirit

The New Testament’s teaching on spiritual gifts is one of the most liberating — and most neglected — resources for thinking about the role of a pastor’s wife. It insists, repeatedly and with considerable emphasis, that God distributes different gifts to different people according to his own purposes — not according to their gender, their husband’s title, or the expectations of their church community.

Paul makes this case most clearly in two letters: Romans 12 and 1 Corinthians 12. Both passages begin by insisting on diversity as part of God’s design — not a problem to be managed, but a feature to be celebrated.

elements.header1: “According to the grace given to us” (v6)
elements.question1: Paul lists seven gifts in this passage — prophecy, service, teaching, exhortation, giving, leading, showing mercy — and says each person has gifts “according to the grace given to us.” The gifts are not chosen by the recipient, earned by effort, or assigned according to social role. They are given by God’s grace. What does it mean that your gifts were given to you by God — not by your husband’s calling, not by your church’s expectations, but by God himself?

deeper.question1: Verse 3 warns against thinking of yourself “more highly than you ought to think” — but it also warns against thinking too lowly, calling each person to think with “sober judgment.” False humility — denying gifts you actually have — is not the same as genuine humility. Is there a gift God has given you that you have been reluctant to name or use? What has made you reluctant?

elements.question1: Paul’s image of the body is specifically designed to address two opposite errors: the parts that feel inferior because they are not more prominent (vv15–16), and the parts that dismiss less visible members as unnecessary (v21). Both errors damage the whole body. Which of these two errors do you think is more common among pastors’ wives — and which are you more prone to yourself?

Verse 7 says the gifts are given “for the benefit of all” — not for the benefit of the individual’s husband, not for the reputation of the church, but for the common good of the whole body. What difference does it make to think of your gifts as held in trust for others, rather than as personal assets or as tools for your husband’s ministry?

deeper.question1: Paul says in verse 18 that “God has placed each one of the members in the body just as he decided.” Your particular combination of gifts, temperament, and experience was placed in the body of Christ by God’s own decision — not by accident, and not by your marital status. Does your current ministry life reflect that placement? Are you in a position to use the gifts God actually gave you — or are you mostly using gifts that were expected of you?

elements.term1: “All pastors’ wives should lead the women’s ministry.”
elements.question1: Is there a standard set of roles that every pastor’s wife should fulfil?
elements.answer1:
No — and the pressure to conform to a single model of the pastor’s wife is one of the most widespread and least examined sources of unhappiness in ministry marriages.

The model varies from denomination to denomination, from culture to culture, and even from congregation to congregation — which is itself a clue that it is culturally constructed rather than biblically mandated. In some traditions, the pastor’s wife is expected to lead worship. In others, she must never be seen on the platform. In some churches she runs the women’s ministry. In others she is expected to stay invisible and domestic. The expectations are often mutually contradictory — which means they cannot all be right.

What the Bible does require of a pastor’s wife is what it requires of every believer: love God, love others, use the gifts you have been given, grow in character, and serve faithfully in the ways that are available to you. Beyond that, the shape of her ministry is between her, her husband, and God — and the congregation’s preferences, however sincerely held, do not override that.

This does not mean ignoring the community or being needlessly provocative. Wisdom about timing and context always matters. But the starting question is not “What does this congregation expect?” It is “What has God given me, and where is he calling me to use it?”

elements.text1: Part 2 — Women Who Led

The Old and New Testaments together give us a wide range of women in significant roles — not as exceptions or anomalies, but as part of the normal landscape of God’s people. Their stories resist the flattening of women’s ministry into a single permitted shape.

Two figures in particular are worth pausing over: Deborah, a judge and military leader in Israel’s most difficult period; and Priscilla, a teacher and church planter in the early church. Neither of them fits neatly into the category of “supporter.” Both are fully themselves, fully gifted, and fully used by God.

elements.header1: Deborah
elements.question1: Deborah is simultaneously a prophet, a judge over all Israel, and a military leader — and the text presents none of this as remarkable or requiring justification. She is simply described as doing what she does. What does the matter-of-fact tone of this passage suggest about how the biblical narrator views her leadership? And what does her story say to women who have been told that significant public leadership is not for them?

deeper.question1: When Barak refuses to go to battle without Deborah (v8), she agrees to go — but tells him plainly that the honour for the victory will go to a woman rather than to him (v9). She does not soften the truth or manage his ego. She is honest, direct, and authoritative. Is there a way in which you have been led to believe that strong, direct leadership is unfeminine or unspiritual? Where did that belief come from?

elements.header1: Priscilla and Aquila
elements.question1: Priscilla and Aquila are mentioned six times in the New Testament, and in four of those six instances Priscilla is named first — an unusual order in the ancient world, where husbands were almost always listed before their wives. In Acts 18:26, she and Aquila together take Apollos — a gifted preacher — aside and explain the faith to him more accurately. What does the New Testament’s consistent presentation of Priscilla suggest about the kind of ministry she exercised?

Priscilla and Aquila are a ministry partnership — they work together in tentmaking, in hospitality, and in teaching. Neither one is simply the support act for the other. How does their example challenge or enrich your understanding of what a ministry marriage can look like?

deeper.question1: Priscilla is operating in a world with strong cultural restrictions on women’s public roles — and she teaches a man. The text records this without apology or qualification. What does this tell you about the relationship between cultural expectations and the gifts and calling that God places on an individual? And what does it say to you personally?

elements.text1: Part 3 — The Quieter Calling

Everything said so far about women’s gifts and leadership could leave a different woman feeling unseen — the woman whose gifts are quieter, whose ministry is less visible, whose calling is genuinely expressed through faithfulness in the background rather than prominence in the foreground.

She needs to hear something clearly: the Bible does not rank gifts by visibility. The body needs every part. And the consistent testimony of the New Testament is that the most quietly exercised gifts — mercy, service, hospitality, encouragement — are not lesser. They are the ones without which the body cannot function at all.

Sister Grace, from our opening story, was not failing to fulfil her potential. She was fulfilling it — in her own shape, according to her own gifts, at the level of depth and consistency that made her genuinely irreplaceable to the people around her. The problem would only arise if she used her own calling as a template to judge Chiamaka — or if Chiamaka used hers to look down on Grace.

Both errors are alive and well in the church. Both are worth resisting.

elements.question1: Paul says in 1 Corinthians 12:22–23 that the parts of the body that seem weaker are actually indispensable, and that the less honourable parts receive more careful attention. He is arguing that the church has things exactly backwards — what it values least is often what it needs most. Where do you see that inversion playing out in the church contexts you know?

deeper.question1: Is there a woman in your church — not a pastor’s wife, perhaps, but an ordinary member — whose quiet faithfulness you have taken for granted or undervalued? What would it look like to honour her, specifically and concretely, this week?

elements.question1: The pressure to conform to a single model of the pastor’s wife can run in two directions: pressure on the quieter woman to be more visible and activist, and pressure on the more public woman to be quieter and more domestic. Have you experienced either of these pressures? Where did it come from — and how did you respond?

elements.term1: “In our family, women don’t take leadership positions.”
elements.question1: What do you do when your family background or ethnic tradition has strong expectations about what women should and should not do — and those expectations conflict with the gifts and calling you sense from God?
elements.answer1:
This is one of the most genuinely difficult tensions in the lives of many Nigerian Christian women — and it deserves a careful answer rather than a quick one.

Family traditions about women’s roles are not always wrong. Many carry real wisdom about honour, community, and relationship. The instinct to respect elders, to maintain family harmony, and to avoid causing unnecessary offence is often a good instinct, and the Bible affirms it in many places.

But family tradition is not Scripture. And when the two conflict — when a family’s cultural expectations would require a woman to suppress or deny gifts that God has clearly given her — the conflict needs to be named honestly, not dissolved by simply giving way.

This does not mean acting provocatively or without wisdom. It means that a woman’s first accountability is to God, who gave her her gifts and her calling. Family expectations — even very strong ones, even from people she loves and respects — are secondary to that. Her husband is her primary human partner in discerning how to navigate the tension. Extended family, however influential, do not have the authority to define her calling.

These conversations are rarely easy. They may need to happen slowly, over time, with patience and love on all sides. But they need to happen — because gifts suppressed for long enough do not stay quietly dormant. They tend to surface in other ways, and not always constructively.

elements.text1: Part 4 — Knowing Your Own Shape

The practical question this session raises is not only theological. It is personal: What are your gifts? What is your calling? What is the shape of the ministry God has given specifically to you — not to the idealised pastor’s wife, not to the woman at the next table at the conference, but to you?

These questions deserve more than a moment’s thought. They deserve the kind of sustained, honest reflection that most women in ministry rarely give themselves permission to undertake. The following questions are designed to help you begin that reflection — not to produce a final answer, but to start a conversation with God and perhaps with one or two trusted others.

elements.question1: Looking back over your life — before ministry, before marriage, as far back as you can remember — what activities, roles, or moments have given you the deepest sense of being in the right place, doing the right thing? What do those moments have in common?
When other people have affirmed gifts in you — not just politely, but with genuine conviction — what have they most consistently named? Does that affirmation match what you sense yourself?

deeper.question1: There is often a gap between the gifts we are using and the gifts we actually have. If you could redesign your ministry involvement from scratch — keeping your marriage, your family, your faith community, but starting fresh with how you spend your time and energy — what would you include that is currently absent? And what would you let go?

elements.question1: Is there a fear — of being too much, of overstepping, of what the congregation would think, of what your in-laws would say — that has kept you from fully engaging with a gift or a sense of calling? Name it as honestly as you can.

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements1: I have a clear sense of the specific gifts God has given me.
My current ministry involvement reflects my actual gifts rather than others’ expectations.
I feel free to exercise my gifts even when they don’t fit the expected model of a pastor’s wife.
I value and celebrate the gifts of other women in ministry, even when they are very different from mine.
I feel supported by my husband in exercising my own calling, distinct from his.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Grace and Chiamaka, from our opening story, were both right — both faithful, both fully themselves. Is there a version of yourself — quieter or more expansive than your current life allows — that you have not yet given permission to exist? What would it take to give her permission?
Of the women in this session — Deborah, Priscilla, Grace, Chiamaka — which one felt most like a recognition? Which felt most like a challenge? What does that tell you?
What is one gift you have been underusing — either because of external pressure, or because you have not quite believed it was really yours? What would it mean to begin using it more intentionally?

elements.text1:
There is no single template for a pastor’s wife in the pages of the New Testament. There is no job description, no uniform, no required gift set. There are women who prophesy and women who serve. Women who teach men and women who teach children. Women who travel and women who stay. Women whose names are listed first and women whose names are not recorded at all.

What they share is not a role. It is a Person — Jesus, in whom they have found their identity, their worth, and their calling. And from that shared foundation, they go in many different directions, with many different gifts, into many different corners of the work.

You are one of those women. God placed specific gifts in you, in your particular combination, for his particular purposes — not the purposes of the denomination, not the expectations of the congregation, not the traditions of your family of origin. His purposes. Yours.

The question is not whether you fit the template. There is no template. The question is whether you are becoming, faithfully and specifically, the woman he made you to be.

elements.question1: What is your challenge for this week?
elements.answer1:
Two things.

First: take the gifts question seriously this week. Set aside an hour — with a notebook, or in prayer, or both — and work through these three questions as honestly as you can:

  • What do I do that seems to come naturally to me, but that others find genuinely difficult?
  • What activities leave me energised rather than depleted, even when they are demanding?
  • What do people consistently come to me for — not because I am the pastor’s wife, but because of who I specifically am?

If you are not sure of your answers, ask someone who knows you well and will tell you the truth.

Second: if you are in a group, come prepared to share one gift you believe God has given you — not a role you have been assigned, but a genuine gift. Say it plainly, without apology. Practise receiving it when others affirm it.

Bring both to the start of Session 2.

elements.text1:
Mama Bello had never stood behind a pulpit. She had never been invited to speak at a conference or lead a women’s retreat. She did not have a title. She had been attending the same church for thirty-one years, and for most of those years she had done one thing with quiet, relentless consistency: she had found the younger women, and she had sat with them.

She sat with them when their husbands were difficult and when their children were sick and when they did not know how to pray. She opened the Bible with them at her kitchen table, unhurried, asking questions more often than she gave answers. She had no programme and no curriculum. She simply gave time — and honesty — and the kind of attention that made people feel they were the only person in the room.

Adaeze — whose story we met in Session 2 of Volume 1 — had been one of Mama Bello’s women. Fifteen years later, Adaeze was doing the same thing with three younger women in her own church, sitting at her own kitchen table, asking the same kind of questions. She had not planned it. She had simply been formed by it.

This is discipleship. Not a programme. A life, passed on.

This session is about what it means to invest in other women — to teach, to disciple, to pass on what has been given to you. It is a calling that every pastor’s wife has access to, whatever her gifts, whatever her formal role, whatever the size of her husband’s church. And it is one of the most significant things she will ever do.
Part 1 — The Titus 2 Pattern

Paul’s letter to Titus contains one of the most direct passages in the New Testament about women teaching women. It is brief — just three verses — but it carries a weight of implication that is easy to miss on a quick reading. It describes not a programme but a pattern: older women, shaped by their own experience of grace, investing that experience in younger women. The church’s health, in Paul’s vision, depends on this pattern functioning well.

elements.header1: “Train the younger women” (v4)
elements.question1: The word translated “train” in verse 4 (sophronizō in Greek) means to bring someone to a place of sound-mindedness, good judgment, and self-control — to help them think and live well. It is not primarily about information transfer. It is about formation. What is the difference between teaching someone information and forming someone’s character and judgment? And which of the two do the younger women around you most need?

deeper.question1: Paul addresses the older women before he says anything about the younger ones — because the discipleship of younger women depends on the older women first being shaped by grace themselves (v3). You cannot give what you do not have. What is the connection between your own continuing growth and your capacity to invest in others? Is there a way in which your investment in others has outrun your own formation?

elements.question1: The content Paul describes — loving husbands and children, self-control, purity, kindness, working at home — is not a list of domestic duties to enforce. It is a picture of a woman whose inner life is ordered and whose relationships are marked by love. Paul says the reason this matters is “so that the word of God may not be discredited” (v5). What is the connection between the quality of women’s discipleship and the credibility of the gospel in a community?

The Titus 2 pattern is described as “older women” teaching “younger women” — but older and younger are relative terms. You do not need to be sixty to have something to pass on. You simply need to be a few steps further along the road. Who are the women in your life who are a few steps behind you — in faith, in marriage, in ministry, in navigating difficulty? Are you investing in any of them intentionally?

deeper.question1: Many women avoid the role of mentor because they feel they are not qualified — not experienced enough, not holy enough, not sorted enough. But the Titus 2 pattern does not require perfection. It requires honesty about the road, including its difficulties. In what ways might your struggles, failures, and hard-won lessons be exactly what a younger woman needs to hear — more than a polished success story would be?

elements.text1: Part 2 — What Is Passed On

Paul’s second letter to Timothy opens with a remarkable passage about intergenerational faith. Timothy is a young church leader, and Paul is writing to encourage him. But before he says anything about Timothy’s ministry or his doctrine, he reaches back two generations — to Timothy’s grandmother Lois and his mother Eunice — and names them as the source of the faith now burning in Timothy.

The faith that changed the world in Timothy’s generation was planted in a household by two women whose names appear nowhere else in Scripture.

elements.header1: Lois and Eunice
elements.question1: Paul says Timothy’s faith “first lived” in his grandmother and his mother before it lived in him. It was transmitted — person to person, generation to generation, in the ordinary fabric of household and relationship. What does this tell you about the significance of faithful, consistent investment in one or two people, even when it feels small and unnoticed?

deeper.question1: Lois and Eunice are not apostles or evangelists. They are not mentioned at a council or a conference. They appear in one brief passage, named as the source of a young man’s faith — and then they are gone from the record. But their investment is still bearing fruit in Timothy’s ministry, and through him in the churches of Asia Minor, and through Paul’s letters in the church of every century since. What does the hiddenness of their influence say about the way God accounts for faithful, quiet discipleship?

elements.question1: Paul tells Timothy to “fan into flame the gift of God” (v6) — a gift that came through the laying on of Paul’s hands, but that was nurtured first by Lois and Eunice. The metaphor of fanning a flame suggests something that requires attention and intention to keep burning. Who has fanned the flame of faith in you? And who are you fanning it in?

Paul’s instruction in 2:2 describes a chain of transmission: what Paul gave to Timothy, Timothy is to entrust to reliable people, who will then be able to teach others. Four generations of investment in one verse. What strikes you about the simplicity and the scope of this model — and what would it look like for it to operate through you?

deeper.question1: Paul says Timothy should entrust what he has received to people who are “faithful” and “able to teach others” — not to the most impressive, the most educated, or the most prominent. Faithfulness and the capacity to pass on are the criteria. Who around you is faithful, and capable of passing on what they receive? Are you investing in them?

elements.term1: “I don’t know enough to teach anyone.”
elements.question1: Do you need formal theological training before you can disciple someone?
elements.answer1:
No — and the expectation that you do is one of the things that keeps the Titus 2 pattern from functioning as it should in many churches.

Discipleship, at its most basic, is not the transfer of theological information. It is the sharing of a life — a life that has been shaped by Scripture, by prayer, by failure and recovery, by the slow work of the Holy Spirit over time. The woman who sits with a younger woman and opens the Bible with her, asks honest questions, shares her own struggles and what she has learned from them, and prays — that woman is doing something that no formal qualification can replace.

This does not mean content does not matter. It does. Growing in your own knowledge of Scripture, your own understanding of the gospel, your own ability to read a passage carefully — all of this makes you a better disciple of others. The investment in your own formation that Paul describes in Titus 2:3 is not optional.

But “I don’t know enough” is almost always a statement about confidence rather than competence. If you have followed Jesus for more than a few years, you know more than a new believer. If you have navigated a difficult marriage, you have something to offer a younger wife. If you have come through grief or illness or a season of doubt and found God faithful, that testimony is worth more in the life of someone facing the same thing than any textbook.

You know enough. Begin.

elements.text1: Part 3 — The First Witnesses

In Session 1 of Volume 1, we noted that the first person Jesus appeared to after the resurrection was a woman — Mary Magdalene — and that he sent her to carry the resurrection news to the disciples. That commissioning deserves a closer look in the context of teaching and witness.

Mary is sent. She goes. She announces. The disciples do not initially believe her (Luke 24:11) — but she says it anyway. She is the first teacher of the central truth of the Christian faith: He is risen.

This pattern — women as primary witnesses and transmitters of gospel truth — is not incidental in Luke’s Gospel. Luke is the most careful historian among the Gospel writers, and he records the women’s role with deliberate precision.

elements.header1: “They remembered his words” (v8)
elements.question1: Before the women can tell anyone anything, they have to remember — to call back to mind what Jesus had said to them in Galilee. Faithful witness begins with faithful memory. What has Jesus said to you — in Scripture, in prayer, in the slow accumulation of a life with him — that you are carrying, that you are responsible for passing on? What do you need to remember, so that you can tell it?

deeper.question1: The women’s testimony is dismissed by the male disciples as “nonsense” (v11) — and yet it is true. They say it anyway. Is there a truth you carry — about God, about grace, about what you have seen him do — that you have been reluctant to speak because you were not sure you would be believed or taken seriously? What would it mean to say it anyway?

elements.question1: Notice who the women tell: “the eleven and all the rest” (v9) — not just women, not just a safe audience, but the whole community of disciples. Their witness is not restricted to a private sphere. What does this suggest about the scope of women’s witness and testimony in the early church — and about the scope of yours?

elements.text1: Part 4 — Teaching in the Grain of Daily Life

The most famous passage in all of Jewish Scripture about the transmission of faith is not about a school or a synagogue. It is about a family, going about ordinary life — walking, sitting, lying down, getting up. The Shema — Israel’s central confession of faith — was to be spoken into the rhythm of daily existence, woven into the texture of ordinary time.

The model is not a programme. It is a practice of presence.

elements.header1: “When you sit… walk… lie down… rise” (v7)
elements.question1: The instruction is to talk about God’s commands in the ordinary moments of daily life — not only in formally religious settings. What does this model of transmission look like for a pastor’s wife investing in other women? What are the “walking and sitting” equivalents — the ordinary shared moments — in which discipleship can happen without a curriculum or a meeting room?

deeper.question1: Mama Bello, from our opening story, had no programme. She had a kitchen table and unhurried time. The Deuteronomy 6 model suggests that the most formative discipleship often happens in the margins rather than in the scheduled hour. What margins do you have — or could you create — in which this kind of unhurried investment might happen?

elements.question1: Verse 6 says the commands must first be “on your heart” before you can speak them to others. The formation of others begins with your own formation. What is God currently writing on your heart — through this study, through Scripture, through your current circumstances — that you might soon be in a position to pass on to someone else?

The Deuteronomy passage speaks of parents and children — but the principle extends further. You are, in some sense, a spiritual parent to the women you invest in. What kind of spiritual parent do you want to be? And what would it mean to be deliberate about it — not just available, but intentional?

deeper.question1: The passage ends with a vision of faith permeating the physical environment — on doorframes, on hands, on foreheads. Faith that is visible in the fabric of daily life, not confined to religious moments. What would it look like for your home, your conversations, your daily rhythms to be that kind of environment for the women you are investing in — a place where faith is simply part of the air?

elements.term1: What does discipleship actually look like week to week?
elements.question1: Discipleship sounds important — but what does it actually look like in practice, for a pastor’s wife with limited time and energy?
elements.answer1:
It does not have to be complicated. In fact, the simpler the better.

  • One-to-one over a regular meeting — coffee, a meal, a walk. A consistent time, roughly fortnightly or monthly, with the same person. Open the Bible together, ask how she is doing, pray before you part. That is enough to start.
  • Reading the same thing — going through a book of the Bible or a short Christian book together, each reading a chapter between meetings, then discussing what struck you. The material gives the conversation structure without requiring you to be an expert.
  • Being available at a specific time — not always-on availability, which is unsustainable, but a named, regular slot when people know they can reach you. This protects your margin while creating genuine access.
  • Inviting in rather than scheduling out — including a younger woman in something you are already doing: accompanying you on a visit, sitting with you during a church event, watching how you handle a pastoral situation. Formation through proximity, not only through formal teaching.
  • Being honest about your own life — sharing what you are learning, what you are struggling with, how you are finding God faithful. Discipleship is not mentorship from a position of arrived perfection. It is one traveller sharing the road with another.

The most important thing is consistency over time. A shallow but consistent investment will almost always outperform an intense but occasional one.

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements1: I am currently investing intentionally in the growth of at least one other woman.
I feel equipped and confident enough to open the Bible with another woman and guide her through it.
I am continuing to grow in my own faith and formation, not just giving out to others.
I have a sense of what I have received from God that I am responsible for passing on.
I believe that my own story — including its difficult parts — has something valuable to offer other women.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Mama Bello changed Adaeze’s life at a kitchen table, without a programme or a title. Is there a woman who has shaped your faith in a similarly quiet, consistent way? What did she do — and what did it give you? Have you thanked her?

Lois and Eunice’s names appear once in Scripture — and the fruit of their investment is still visible two thousand years later. What do you want to pass on? If you imagine a woman — your daughter in the faith, not necessarily your biological daughter — looking back in twenty years and naming what she received from you, what do you most want her to be able to say?
Is there a specific woman — someone you already know — in whom you sense God calling you to invest more intentionally? What is one step you could take this week to begin or deepen that investment?

elements.text1:
You have received something. A faith that was planted in you, nurtured in you, kept alive in you — by Scripture, by prayer, by the Spirit of God, and almost certainly by the faithfulness of other people. Some of them are named in your memory. Some, like Lois and Eunice, are the unnamed ones whose consistency made everything else possible.

You are not at the end of that chain. You are in the middle of it. What was given to you is not yours to keep. It is yours to pass on — in whatever shape, at whatever pace, through whatever relationship God places in your hands.

The kitchen table is enough. The honest conversation is enough. The open Bible and the unhurried question are enough. Mama Bello knew that. Lois knew it. Eunice knew it.

Now it is your turn.

elements.question1: What is your challenge for this week?
elements.answer1:
Three things — choose at least one.

If you are not yet discipling anyone: identify one woman this week — someone you already know, someone a few steps behind you in some area of life or faith — and take one concrete step toward her. Send a message. Suggest a coffee. Make it simple and low-pressure. You are not proposing a formal programme. You are offering friendship with intention.

If you are already investing in someone: this week, before you meet with her, spend ten minutes asking God what she most needs from you right now — not what you planned to say, but what she needs. Then ask her, at the start of your time together: “How are you — really?” And give her enough time to answer.

For everyone: write down the name of the person who most shaped your faith — your Mama Bello, your Lois, your Eunice. If they are still alive, send them a message this week telling them what they gave you. If they are not, write it down anyway — and let it remind you of the weight of what you carry, and what you are responsible for passing on.

Bring one thing from this week to share at the start of Session 3.

elements.question1: It’s worth having these passages open as you work through this session.

elements.text1:
Hauwa had lived next door to the widow for two years before she actually saw her.

She had noticed her, of course — a small woman in her sixties, always in the same faded wrapper, collecting water from the compound tap early in the morning before anyone else was up. She knew her name was Mama Idris. She knew her husband had died three years ago and that her children were somewhere in Lagos. She had greeted her at the gate.

But one Thursday morning, returning from the market, Hauwa found Mama Idris sitting on the step outside her door, not moving. She stopped. She sat down beside her on the step. She asked nothing — just sat.

After a long time, Mama Idris said: ‘Today is the anniversary.’

They sat together for another hour. Hauwa missed the women’s prayer meeting she had been hurrying back for. She did not feel guilty about it.

That evening, thinking about it, she realised something had shifted. She had been doing a great deal of ministry — attending, organising, supporting, being present at the right events. But she had been doing most of it at a slight distance from actual people. The hour on the step with Mama Idris had felt different. Less managed. More real.

She had not known what to say. She had not needed to. She had simply been there — and that, it turned out, was the thing most needed.

This session is about pastoral care: not as a professional skill, not as a ministry programme, but as a posture of the heart. It is the capacity to see the people others overlook, to feel what they feel, to be present without agenda, and to act — concretely, consistently — on behalf of those who cannot act for themselves.

It is, the Bible suggests, close to the heart of God.
Part 1 — Who Is My Neighbour?

The parable of the Good Samaritan is one of the most familiar stories in the Gospels — which makes it one of the most difficult to hear freshly. We know the outcome before we begin. The Samaritan helps. The priest and the Levite do not.

What is easily missed is the social structure of the story. The priest and the Levite are not villains. They are religious professionals with responsibilities, schedules, and legitimate reasons to be careful about ritual contamination. The Samaritan is a social outsider — ethnically and religiously suspect, someone a Jewish audience would expect to be the failure of the story, not its hero.

Jesus chooses him deliberately. And the question he ends with is not ‘What should you think about your neighbour?’ It is: ‘Which one of these three acted like a neighbour?’

Neighbour, in Jesus’ vocabulary, is not a category you belong to. It is something you do.

elements.passageText1:
25Now an expert in religious law stood up to test Jesus, saying, ‘Teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life?’ 26He said to him, ‘What is written in the law? How do you understand it?’ 27The expert answered, ‘Love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, with all your strength, and with all your mind, and love your neighbor as yourself.’ 28Jesus said to him, ‘You have answered correctly; do this, and you will live.’

29But the expert, wanting to justify himself, said to Jesus, ‘And who is my neighbor?’ 30Jesus replied, ‘A man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and fell into the hands of robbers, who stripped him, beat him up, and went off, leaving him half dead. 31Now by chance a priest was going down that road, but when he saw the injured man he passed by on the other side. 32So too a Levite, when he came up to the place and saw him, passed by on the other side. 33But a Samaritan who was traveling came to where the injured man was, and when he saw him, he felt compassion for him. 34He went up to him and bandaged his wounds, pouring oil and wine on them. Then he put him on his own animal, brought him to an inn, and took care of him. 35The next day he took out two silver coins and gave them to the innkeeper, saying, ‘Take care of him, and whatever else you spend, I will repay you when I come back this way.’ 36Which of these three do you think became a neighbor to the man who fell into the hands of the robbers?’ 37The expert in religious law said, ‘The one who showed mercy to him.’ So Jesus said to him, ‘Go and do the same.’


(NET — see context ↗)

elements.header1: "He had compassion" (v33)
elements.question1: The Samaritan sees the wounded man, and something moves in him — the Greek word (splanchnizomai) describes a physical reaction, a visceral response deep in the body. It is the same word used of Jesus when he sees the crowds "like sheep without a shepherd" (Matthew 9:36). Compassion, in the Bible, is not a sentiment. It is something felt in the body that produces action. When did you last feel something like that — a response to someone’s need that was physical before it was planned? What happened next?

deeper.question1: The priest and the Levite "passed by on the other side" — an active choice to increase the distance. It is possible to pass by on the other side while remaining very busy with religious activity. In what ways might the demands of ministry life actually make it easier to pass by on the other side — to be so occupied with the programme that you miss the person on the road? Where do you see that risk in your own life?

elements.question1: The Samaritan’s care is practical and costly: oil and wine for the wounds, a donkey to carry the man, an inn, money, a promise to return. He does not offer sympathy and move on. He rearranges his journey and his resources around someone else’s need. What is the difference between sympathy — feeling sorry for someone — and the kind of care the Samaritan shows? And which of the two is more common in church life?

Jesus ends the parable not with "now you know who your neighbour is" but with "go and do likewise" (v37). The question shifts from definition to action. Who is the person on the road in your world right now — the one who is wounded, overlooked, in need of practical care — and what would "go and do likewise" mean for you this week?

deeper.question1: The lawyer who asked the original question was trying to "justify himself" (v29) — to draw a boundary around "neighbour" that would let him off the hook. We do the same: we define our responsibilities narrowly so that most people fall outside them. Where do you draw the boundary of your pastoral responsibility — and does Jesus’ answer in this parable push that boundary further than you are comfortable with?

elements.text1: Part 2 — Mourning With Those Who Mourn

Romans 12 contains one of the most concentrated passages of practical instruction in Paul’s letters — a rapid sequence of short commands about how the community of God’s people is to treat one another and the wider world. At its centre is a pair of phrases that together describe the most fundamental posture of genuine pastoral care.

It is not advice. It is not problem-solving. It is presence: rejoice with those who rejoice, mourn with those who mourn.

The mourning side of that pair is the harder one. Rejoicing with those who rejoice asks only for generosity and the absence of envy. Mourning with those who mourn asks for something more costly: the willingness to enter into someone else’s grief, to sit in it with them, to resist the urge to explain it or fix it or move them through it more quickly than they are ready to go.

elements.passageText1:
9Love must be without hypocrisy. Abhor what is evil, cling to what is good. 10Be devoted to one another with mutual love, showing eagerness in honoring one another. 11Do not lag in zeal, be enthusiastic in spirit, serve the Lord. 12Rejoice in hope, endure in suffering, persist in prayer. 13Contribute to the needs of the saints, pursue hospitality. 14Bless those who persecute you, bless and do not curse. 15Rejoice with those who rejoice, weep with those who weep. 16Live in harmony with one another; do not be haughty but associate with the lowly. Do not be conceited.


(NET — see context ↗)

elements.header1: "Mourn with those who mourn" (v15)
elements.question1: Mourning with someone does not mean sharing their despair or losing your own footing. It means being willing to be present in their pain without rushing to resolve it. What makes that difficult — for you personally, and for church culture more broadly? What do we tend to do instead of simply staying?

deeper.question1: In many Nigerian church contexts, grief is expected to be short, faith is expected to produce visible victory, and sustained sorrow can be read as a lack of trust in God. How does that expectation affect the way grieving people experience the church — and what would it look like to create a different kind of space, where mourning is held with patience rather than hurried toward resolution?

elements.question1: Verse 16 instructs: "Do not be haughty, but associate with the lowly. Do not be conceited." In a church setting, the "lowly" are often the people with the least status, the least influence, the least to offer the ministry in return. Who are those people in your church context — and how much of your pastoral energy currently reaches them?

Paul begins this section in verse 9 with: "Love must be without hypocrisy." The Greek word for hypocrisy (anupokritos) means literally "without a mask" — love that is not performed for an audience but genuine at the level of the will. What is the difference between pastoral care that is genuine and pastoral care that is performed — and how do you tell the difference in yourself?

deeper.question1: Hauwa, in our opening story, missed a prayer meeting to sit on a step with a grieving widow. Most ministry cultures would regard the prayer meeting as the more important commitment. Was she right to stay? And what does her choice reveal about the relationship between organised religious activity and the kind of love Paul is describing in this passage?

elements.term1: "Pastoral care is the pastor’s job, not mine."
elements.question1: Is it appropriate for a pastor’s wife to be involved in pastoral care — or is that overstepping?
elements.answer1:
Pastoral care — in the broad sense of caring for the souls and bodies of people in need — is not the exclusive property of the ordained minister. It is the responsibility of the whole body of Christ.

The New Testament uses the language of ‘one another’ more than fifty times to describe how believers are to treat each other: love one another, bear one another’s burdens, encourage one another, confess to one another, pray for one another. None of these instructions is restricted to those with formal ministry roles.

A pastor’s wife is, by the nature of her position, often more accessible to certain people than her husband is — particularly to women who would find it difficult to share certain things with a male pastor. The woman who has just miscarried. The wife whose marriage is struggling. The young woman who has been abused. The older woman who is dying and afraid. These women need someone — and a pastor’s wife who is present, trustworthy, and genuinely caring may be the most important person in the church for them.

This does not mean taking on more than you can carry, or operating without boundaries, or substituting yourself for professional help when it is needed. Wisdom about limits is part of good pastoral care. But the instinct to withdraw from care because ‘that’s the pastor’s job’ can leave some of the most vulnerable people in a congregation without anyone.

You have permission — and more than permission — to care.

elements.text1: Part 3 — Pure Religion

James is the most practically minded writer in the New Testament. He is suspicious of faith that produces no visible fruit, of religion that fills the mouth but empties the hands. His definition of genuine religion — of what God actually considers pure and undefiled — is startling in its simplicity and its specificity. It has nothing to do with correct doctrine or impressive worship. It has to do with two categories of people: orphans and widows.

In the ancient world, and in many parts of Nigeria today, orphans and widows are among the most economically and socially vulnerable members of a community. They are people without the natural protection of a husband and father — people who depend on the generosity and justice of others for their basic survival. The prophets of Israel returned to them again and again as the test of a community’s faithfulness. So does James.

elements.passageText1:
27Pure and undefiled religion before God the Father is this: to care for orphans and widows in their misfortune and to keep oneself unstained by the world.


(NET — see context ↗)

elements.question1: James says that religion God considers "pure and undefiled" consists of visiting orphans and widows in their affliction, and keeping oneself unstained by the world. These two things — outward care for the vulnerable and inward moral integrity — are placed side by side as the definition of genuine faith. What does it say about the character of God that care for the vulnerable is, in James’s view, the mark of true religion?

deeper.question1: The word "visit" (episkeptomai) in James 1:27 is the same word used of God "visiting" his people in the Exodus — it means to come close, to attend to, to actively investigate and respond to a need. It is not distant concern. It is presence. Who are the "orphans and widows" — the most structurally vulnerable people — in your specific community? And what would it mean to visit them in that active, attending sense?

elements.passageText1:
6No, this is the kind of fast I want. I want you to remove the sinful chains, to tear away the ropes of the burdensome yoke, to set free the oppressed, and to break every burdensome yoke. 7I want you to share your food with the hungry and to provide shelter for homeless, oppressed people. When you see someone naked, clothe him! Don’t turn your back on your own flesh and blood!


(NET — see context ↗)

elements.question1: Isaiah 58 is God’s response to a people who are fasting and praying and asking why God does not seem to hear them. His answer is searching: the fast he chooses is not an empty stomach but justice — loosing bonds of wickedness, freeing the oppressed, sharing bread with the hungry, bringing the homeless into your house. What is the relationship between the practices of personal piety — prayer, fasting, worship — and care for the vulnerable? Can one genuinely substitute for the other?

Isaiah’s list in verses 6–7 is concrete and practical: food, shelter, clothing, solidarity with kin. There is nothing abstract about it. As a pastor’s wife, you are likely well-placed to know who in your congregation and community lacks these things. What systems — formal or informal — exist in your church for identifying and responding to concrete material need? And where are the gaps?

deeper.question1: Isaiah says that when Israel practises this kind of justice, "your light will break out like the dawn" and "your healing will spring up quickly" (v8). He connects the church’s witness in the world directly to its practice of justice and care. What does this suggest about the relationship between a church’s pastoral and social engagement and its capacity to reach its community with the gospel?

elements.text1: Part 4 — Jesus and the Widow of Nain

Luke 7 contains a brief, luminous account that is easy to pass over — a healing miracle, but unlike most of the others. The woman at the centre of it does not ask for anything. She does not reach out to touch Jesus’ garment or send a message through friends or cry out from the roadside. She is simply walking — carrying her grief, heading toward a burial, not looking for anything because she has learned, perhaps, that there is nothing left to look for.

Jesus sees her. He is moved. He acts.

This encounter is a picture of what a pastoral heart looks like at its most instinctive — not responding to a request, not meeting an articulated need, but seeing someone who has not asked, and moving toward them anyway.

elements.passageText1:
11Soon afterward Jesus went to a town called Nain, and his disciples and a large crowd went with him. 12As he approached the town gate, a dead man was being carried out — the only son of his mother, and she was a widow. A large crowd from the town was with her. 13When the Lord saw her, he had compassion for her and said to her, "Do not weep." 14Then he came forward and touched the bier, and those carrying it stood still. He said, "Young man, I say to you, get up!" 15So the dead man sat up and began to speak, and Jesus gave him back to his mother. 16Fear seized them all, and they began to glorify God, saying, "A great prophet has appeared among us!" and "God has come to help his people!" 17This report about him spread throughout Judea and all the surrounding countryside.


(NET — see context ↗)

elements.header1: "When the Lord saw her, he had compassion on her" (v13)
elements.question1: The widow has not approached Jesus. She has not asked for anything. She is simply visible — a woman in grief, walking toward a burial. And Jesus sees her. The first thing that happens in this story is not a miracle — it is sight. He looks at her and feels something. Who are the people in your world who have not asked for anything — who are simply walking, quietly, carrying something heavy — but whom God might be inviting you to see?

deeper.question1: Jesus’ first words to the widow are not a question or a request. They are a gift: "Do not weep" (v13) — not as a command to suppress grief, but as a promise that there is reason not to. He speaks before he acts. Is there someone in your life to whom you could speak a word of genuine comfort — not a platitude, but a specific, true, personal word — before anything else? What would that word be?

elements.question1: This widow is doubly vulnerable: she has lost her husband, and now her only son — her sole remaining source of financial and social protection in a society that provided no other safety net for women alone. Jesus understands the full weight of her situation, not just its emotional dimension. What does it mean to see someone’s need fully — not only the visible grief, but the practical, structural vulnerability underneath it? How does that kind of seeing change the nature of your care?

After the miracle, verse 17 says "this report about him went out through all Judea and all the surrounding region." An act of compassion toward one overlooked woman in a small town became news across the region. It changed how people understood who Jesus was. What does this suggest about the reach of a single act of genuine pastoral care — and about the witness it can be to the character of God?

deeper.question1: Jesus’ compassion for the widow of Nain is, ultimately, a window into the character of God — a God who sees the overlooked, moves toward the grieving, and acts on behalf of those who have not asked because they did not know it was possible. How does this image of God shape the way you understand your own pastoral calling? What would it mean to be, in your small corner of the world, a reflection of that kind of seeing and moving?

elements.question1: Pastoral care involves absorbing other people’s pain. How do you do that without being destroyed by it?
elements.answer1:
This is one of the most important practical questions in ministry life — and it rarely gets an honest answer.

The truth is that sustained pastoral care does cost something. You cannot sit with grief, hold trauma, witness suffering, and carry confidences without being affected. Pretending otherwise is not faith — it is denial, and it tends to produce the burnout and emotional numbness that make genuine care impossible over time.

Some things that help:

Limit what you carry alone. Confidentiality is important, but it does not mean carrying everything by yourself. A trusted person — a supervisor, a spiritual director, a wise friend outside the congregation — with whom you can process what you are holding without breaking confidence is not a luxury. It is essential.

Bring it to God specifically. Not general prayer, but bringing the specific weight of what you are carrying — the face, the situation, the grief — and leaving it with him. ‘I carried this today. I give it back to you now.’ That practice, done consistently, makes a real difference.

Know your limits. There are situations — severe mental illness, domestic violence, addiction, trauma — that require professional help. Knowing when to refer is not a failure of pastoral care. It is part of it. Trying to carry what you are not equipped to carry does not help the person — it puts them in the care of someone who is out of their depth, and it damages you.

Rest between. As Session 6 of Volume 1 established — rest is not optional. The capacity to be genuinely present to people in pain depends on having space to recover between those encounters.

You cannot pour from an empty vessel. Caring for yourself is part of caring for others.

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements1: I am genuinely attentive to the people around me who are suffering, even when they have not asked for help.
I feel equipped to sit with someone in grief or difficulty without trying to fix it or rush them through it.
My pastoral care reaches people at the margins of my church community, not only those who are visible and connected.
I have a way of processing the weight of other people’s pain without being overwhelmed by it.
I believe that care for the vulnerable is as central to my calling as prayer or Bible study.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Hauwa saw Mama Idris only when she stopped hurrying and sat down. Is there someone in your world — in your congregation, your neighbourhood, your compound — whom you have been noticing without yet seeing? What would it mean to stop and sit down?

Of the images of pastoral care in this session — the Samaritan’s costly rearrangement, Paul’s unmasked mourning, James’s concrete visiting, Jesus’ unsolicited compassion — which one challenged you most? What do you think that challenge is about?

James says that pure religion includes visiting orphans and widows in their affliction. Who are the most structurally vulnerable people in your specific context — the people with the least protection, the fewest resources, the smallest voice? What is one concrete thing you could do this month to move toward them?

elements.text1:
Pastoral care is not a programme. It is a posture — a way of moving through the world with your eyes open and your heart available, alert to the person on the road, attentive to the woman on the step, willing to stop for the funeral procession no one expected you to notice.

It will cost you time. It will cost you margin. It will sometimes cost you the meeting you were hurrying back to. And it will ask of you something that no training fully prepares you for: the willingness to be present to pain without immediately trying to fix it.

But it is among the most Christlike things you will ever do. Jesus saw the widow of Nain before she saw him. He moved toward her before she moved toward him. He gave before she asked.

You are not Jesus. You cannot raise the dead. But you can stop. You can sit down. You can say: I see you. You can stay long enough for it to mean something.

That is where pastoral care begins. And sometimes, it is where it ends — and that is enough.

elements.question1: What is your challenge for this week?
elements.answer1:
Two things this week.

First: identify one person in your congregation or community who is structurally vulnerable — a widow, an elderly person living alone, a woman whose husband has left, a family in genuine financial difficulty — and do one concrete thing for them. Not a prayer on their behalf from a distance. Something practical: a visit, a meal, a phone call that is long enough to actually listen, an envelope.

Second: the next time someone tells you about a difficulty they are facing — in person, by phone, in a message — resist the urge to give advice or find the silver lining. Simply say: ‘That sounds really hard. Tell me more.’ Then listen. Notice how long you can hold the space before the urge to fix or reassure becomes overwhelming — and notice what happens when you don’t give in to it.

Bring one reflection from either of these things to the start of Session 4.

elements.question1: It’s worth having these passages open as you work through this session.

elements.text1:
The message came through a third party, as these things often do.

Sister Comfort — a long-standing member of the women’s fellowship, someone Taiwo had considered a friend — had apparently been telling other women that Taiwo was proud, that she thought herself better than everyone else since her husband became senior pastor, and that she had changed. The women who heard it were divided: some dismissed it, some repeated it, and at least one came directly to Taiwo to tell her what was being said. That woman meant well. The telling made everything worse.

Taiwo spent three days in a state she could only describe as a slow fire. She was not sure which was stronger: the hurt of being misrepresented, the anger at the disloyalty, or the cold suspicion — which she hated herself for feeling — that somewhere underneath Sister Comfort’s words there might be a grain of truth she would have to examine.

She did not know what to do. Confronting Sister Comfort directly felt dangerous. Ignoring it felt like surrender. Talking to her husband about it would drag him into something he did not need. Talking to anyone else in the church felt like adding to the very problem she was trying to stop.

She prayed. The fire did not go out immediately. But she stayed at the prayer long enough to ask a question she had not asked before: Lord, what do you want to do with this — not just with Sister Comfort, but with me?

That question, it turned out, was the beginning of the way through.

This session is about conflict: the gossip that travels through a congregation before you know it has started, the manipulation that is hard to name because it is always deniable, the misunderstanding that calcifies into resentment, the family pressure that arrives from outside the church and lands inside your marriage. These are not exotic problems. They are the ordinary furniture of ministry life. And the Bible has more to say about navigating them than most churches teach.
Part 1 — When Someone Has Wronged You

Matthew 18 contains the most direct teaching Jesus gives about what to do when a fellow believer has wronged you. It is a passage that is frequently quoted and less frequently followed — partly because it asks for something that feels unnatural and costly, and partly because it runs against the instinct, common in many cultures, to manage conflict indirectly rather than address it face to face.

Jesus’ model is disarmingly simple. And it is worth noticing who he is addressing: not a community leader managing church disputes, but any person who has been wronged. The responsibility to act belongs to the one who has been hurt.

elements.header1: "Go and show him his fault — just between the two of you" (v15)
elements.question1: Jesus’ first instruction is to go directly to the person — privately, one to one, before involving anyone else. This immediately cuts across the most common response to being wronged, which is to process it with other people first. Why do you think Jesus insists on the private, direct conversation as the first step? What does skipping it tend to produce?

deeper.question1: The goal Jesus names is not vindication or an apology — it is to "win your brother" (v15). The purpose of the conversation is restoration of relationship, not the establishment of who was right. How does that goal change the way you would prepare for and enter a difficult conversation with someone who has hurt you? What would you need to let go of in order to approach it that way?

elements.question1: Jesus provides a graduated process: first one to one, then one or two witnesses, then the church. Each step is an escalation of last resort, taken only when the previous step has failed. What does this graduated structure say about the value Jesus places on privacy and proportionality in conflict — and how does that contrast with the way conflict tends to spread in church communities?

In practice, a pastor’s wife is rarely able to follow Matthew 18 in a straightforward way — her husband’s position complicates every step, and what might be a private matter between two individuals can quickly become a church politics issue. Given that complexity, what principles from this passage can still guide the way you approach conflict, even when the full process is not available to you?

deeper.question1: Taiwo’s instinct was that confronting Sister Comfort directly felt dangerous. That instinct is understandable — direct confrontation carries real risks in many relational and cultural contexts. But what are the costs of never addressing things directly? What tends to happen to unaddressed hurt over time — in you, and in the relationship?

elements.text1: Part 2 — Gossip and the Slow Fire

Gossip is not a minor vice. In the New Testament it appears in lists alongside sexual immorality, idolatry, and murder — not because it is equally harmful in every instance, but because it is a symptom of the same disordered heart, and because its effects on community are deeply destructive. Proverbs, with its characteristic concrete imagery, describes what gossip does with particular vividness.

For a pastor’s wife, gossip presents a specific double problem: she is more likely than most to be the subject of it, and she has access — through her husband’s confidences and her own pastoral relationships — to information that could fuel it. Both vulnerabilities require attention.

elements.header1: "Without wood, a fire goes out" (v20)
elements.question1: Proverbs uses three images for gossip: a fire without wood, a quarrel without a whisperer, and charcoal on embers. All three are about the relationship between fuel and flame — remove the fuel, and the fire dies. What is the practical implication of this image for how you respond when gossip about someone — or about yourself — reaches you? What does it mean to refuse to be fuel?

deeper.question1: Verse 22 says the words of a gossip are like delicious morsels that go down into the inner parts — there is something genuinely appealing about receiving gossip, even when we know we should not. Honest self-examination matters here: are there situations in which you have enjoyed receiving information about others that you should not have encouraged? What made it appealing in that moment?

elements.question1: A pastor’s wife often hears things — about people in the congregation, about situations in other churches, about her husband’s confidential pastoral conversations — that she carries alone. The temptation to share even a fraction of that, dressed as a prayer request or a general concern, is real. What disciplines or commitments help you guard what has been entrusted to you — and what do you do when the weight of carrying it alone becomes heavy?
When gossip is circulating about you — as it was about Taiwo — what are the options available to you, and what are the costs and benefits of each? How do you decide which to pursue?

deeper.question1: Taiwo eventually asked: "Lord, what do you want to do with this — not just with Sister Comfort, but with me?" That question opened the possibility that the gossip, however unjust, might contain something worth examining. Is there a criticism or a piece of gossip about you — past or present — that, when you strip away the malice or the distortion, might carry a grain of truth worth sitting with? What would honest self-examination in that direction look like?

elements.question1: Is sharing true but private information about someone acceptable — especially in a church context?
elements.answer1:
This is one of the most common rationalisations for gossip — and one of the most damaging.

Truth is not the only criterion for whether information should be shared. The other questions are: Does the person sharing it have a right to share it? Does the person receiving it need to know it? Will sharing it serve the other person’s good — or will it primarily serve the speaker’s need to feel informed, connected, or significant?

In a church context especially, information shared under the guise of prayer or pastoral concern can function as gossip even when every detail is accurate. "Please pray for Brother Emeka — his marriage is in trouble" may be factually true. It may still be a violation of Emeka’s privacy, destructive to his reputation, and entirely unnecessary for the spiritual welfare of the person being told.

The test is not "Is this true?" It is "Is this mine to share? Does this person need to know? Will sharing this build up or tear down?"

A pastor’s wife who handles information with consistent discretion — who is known to be someone things do not travel through — builds a trust that makes her far more useful to people in genuine need. People bring their real problems to those they know will not circulate them. That trust, once built, is one of the most valuable things you have.

elements.text1: Part 3 — Overcoming Evil with Good

Romans 12 ends with one of the most demanding practical instructions in Paul’s letters — a section about how believers are to respond to those who have wronged them. It immediately follows the section on mourning with those who mourn and loving without hypocrisy. Paul moves without pause from the warmth of community care to the harder territory of response to enemies. He seems to regard them as part of the same subject.

His instruction is not passive resignation. It is an active, strategic, morally demanding choice — one that requires more strength than retaliation, not less.

elements.header1: "Do not be overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good" (v21)
elements.question1: Paul’s final instruction is a reversal of the expected logic: instead of evil overcoming you, you overcome evil — but with good, not with matching force. This is not passivity. It is a different kind of agency. What does it look like, concretely, to overcome evil with good in the specific context of church conflict — gossip, manipulation, or being publicly misrepresented?

deeper.question1: Verse 19 grounds the instruction in the character of God: "Vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord." Leaving repayment to God is not the same as pretending the wrong did not happen. It is an act of trust — that God sees, that God is just, and that justice does not depend on you securing it yourself. How difficult do you find that trust in practice — especially when the person who has wronged you is continuing in the congregation, apparently unaffected?

elements.question1: Verse 20 quotes Proverbs 25:21–22 about feeding your enemy when they are hungry — meeting need with generosity rather than withholding out of resentment. This is an active, costly choice. Can you think of a situation — real or hypothetical — in which you would be called to do something genuinely good for someone who has genuinely wronged you? What would that require of you?

Paul says in verse 26: "Be angry and do not sin; do not let the sun go down on the cause of your anger." He does not say anger is wrong — he assumes it will happen. The instruction is about what you do with it: do not let it ferment. What is the difference between anger that is acknowledged and addressed, and anger that is suppressed or nursed? And what does anger that is not addressed tend to become over time?

deeper.question1: Verse 31 lists the things to "put away": bitterness, rage, anger, quarrelling, slander, malice. Several of these can exist quietly, beneath the surface, never quite erupting but never quite resolved — what might be called a slow bitterness. Is there a relationship in your ministry life — past or present — in which something like that has settled in? What would it take to bring it to the surface and deal with it honestly?

elements.text1: Part 4 — Family, In-Laws, and the Pressures That Come from Outside

Not all the conflict a pastor’s wife navigates comes from within the congregation. Some of the most persistent and painful pressure comes from outside — from extended family, from in-laws, from the cultural expectations of her own community of origin. These pressures are often unspoken, rarely directly addressed, and yet consistently felt: in decisions about children, about money, about where to live, about what kind of ministry wife she should be.

Joseph’s story in Genesis does not speak directly to in-laws. But it is the most sustained account in the Old Testament of a person who was consistently wronged — by family, by those he served, by circumstances that seemed to contradict every promise God had made — and who came through it not bitter but enlarged. His response to his brothers at the end of the story is one of the most searching passages in the whole Bible on the subject of forgiveness and the long view.

elements.header1: "You intended to harm me, but God intended it for good" (v20)
elements.question1: Joseph does not pretend his brothers did not harm him — he acknowledges it plainly: "you intended to harm me." But he places that harm inside a larger story: what they intended for evil, God used for good. This is not a denial of the wound. It is a refusal to let the wound have the last word. What does it take to arrive at that kind of perspective — and do you think it is possible before the good has become visible?

deeper.question1: Joseph’s brothers are afraid even after their father has died — they assume that Joseph’s kindness was performed for Jacob’s benefit and that now the real response will come (v15). Long-term harm can make people unable to receive genuine goodness when it arrives. Is there a relationship in your life — with a family member, an in-law, a church member — where trust has been so damaged that genuine reconciliation feels impossible? What would the first small step toward it look like — not full restoration, but one movement in that direction?

elements.question1: Joseph weeps when his brothers speak to him (v17) — the emotion is real, not managed. And then he speaks kindly to them and provides for them (v21). The forgiveness is not cold or distant. It is warm and costly and expressed in practical generosity. What does this suggest about what forgiveness actually involves — and what it does not require?

elements.term1: Extended family, in-laws, and the ministry household
elements.question1: How do you navigate the specific pressures that come from in-laws and extended family in a traditional Nigerian context — expectations about children, money, time, and what kind of wife and minister’s wife you should be?
elements.answer1:
This is one of the areas where many pastors’ wives carry weight that is rarely acknowledged in church settings — because the pressure comes from outside the church, and yet it lands directly inside the marriage and the ministry.

In many Nigerian communities, a daughter-in-law is understood to belong, in a meaningful sense, to her husband’s family — not only to her husband. Her choices, her appearance, her fertility, her relationship to her own family of origin, and her involvement in ministry may all be subject to comment, expectation, and sometimes direct interference from in-laws who consider these things their legitimate concern.

When those expectations are wise and loving, they are genuinely a gift — the extended family as a resource of support, wisdom, and community. But when they conflict with a couple’s sense of calling, with the wife’s sense of identity, or with the basic functioning of the household, they require careful navigation.

A few principles that may help:

  • The primary household is the couple’s. Genesis 2:24 — "a man shall leave his father and mother and be joined to his wife" — establishes a primary loyalty. Extended family matters enormously, but it is secondary to the marriage. A husband who allows his family of origin to consistently override his wife’s voice is not honouring the marriage covenant.
  • Name the pressure, don’t absorb it silently. Unspoken resentment toward in-laws tends to leak — into the marriage, into parenting, into the ministry. The couple who can talk honestly about external pressures — without one defending the family and the other attacking them — is in a much stronger position than the couple who pretend the pressure is not there.
  • Choose your battles with wisdom. Not every expectation needs to be resisted. Some things — attendance at family events, certain expressions of respect — cost little and preserve relationship. Others — decisions about where to live, how many children to have, whether the wife works outside the home — are genuinely the couple’s to make. Knowing the difference matters.
  • Bring it to God together. Extended family pressure is a spiritual issue as well as a relational one. The couple that prays together about it — naming the specific pressures, asking for wisdom, seeking God’s direction — is less likely to be divided by it.

This is hard territory. You are not the first pastor’s wife to navigate it, and you will not be the last. But you do not have to navigate it alone — and you do not have to resolve it all at once.
