elements.question1: Extended family pressure often operates through indirect means — a comment made to your husband rather than to you, an expectation communicated through a third party, a silence that is more eloquent than words. How do you recognise manipulation or pressure when it arrives in these indirect forms — and what makes it so difficult to respond to clearly?

deeper.question1: Joseph’s ability to say "God intended it for good" came at the end of a very long story — after years of slavery, imprisonment, and waiting. He could not have said it at the beginning. Is there a long-running difficulty — a family pressure, a ministry disappointment, a relationship that has been a source of pain — that you are still in the middle of, where the good God is working is not yet visible? What does Joseph’s story say to you from the middle of the story, before the end is clear?

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements1: I have a clear sense of how to respond when I am wronged — rather than simply reacting.
I am able to handle confidential information with consistent discretion.
I am not currently carrying unresolved bitterness or resentment toward anyone in my church or family.
I feel able to talk honestly with my husband about pressures that come from his family or mine.
I believe that God can use difficult relationships and conflicts for purposes I cannot yet see.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Taiwo’s way through began when she shifted her question from "What should I do about Sister Comfort?" to "Lord, what do you want to do with this — in me?" Is there a current conflict or difficulty where you need to make that same shift? What might God want to do in you through it — not just through the other person?

Joseph said: "You intended it for harm; God intended it for good." Is there a past wound — from a church member, from a family member, from a season of ministry — that you have not yet been able to hold inside that larger story? What would it mean to begin to try?

Of the specific conflicts this session has addressed — gossip, manipulation, being misunderstood, extended family pressure — which one is most live for you right now? What is one concrete step you could take this week — toward God, toward the other person, or toward honest conversation with your husband — that would move you, however slightly, in the right direction?

elements.text1:
Conflict in ministry is not a sign that something has gone wrong with your faith or your church. It is a sign that you are in close relationship with imperfect people — which is the only kind of people there are, including you.

The Bible does not promise conflict-free ministry. It promises a God who is present in the conflict, who sees the wrong done to you, who will settle accounts in his own time, and who is — even now, even in the middle of the slow fire — doing something in you that he could not do any other way.

Taiwo’s three days of slow fire were not wasted. They produced a question she had not thought to ask. The question produced honesty. The honesty produced a kind of freedom she had not expected.

That is usually how it works. The conflict that feels most like an obstacle is often the thing God is most at work in. Not around it. Not despite it. In it.

Stay in the question. Let him work.

elements.question1: What is your challenge for this week?
elements.answer1:
Two things — one outward, one inward.

Outward: Is there a relationship in your life — with a church member, a family member, an in-law — that has been strained for some time and that you have been avoiding? This week, take one small step toward it. Not a full reconciliation conversation — just one step. A greeting that is warmer than usual. A message that does not pretend everything is fine but opens a door. A prayer for that person by name, said aloud, genuinely.

Inward: Spend fifteen minutes this week with Ephesians 4:31 — "Put away from you all bitterness, rage, anger, quarrelling, and slander, together with all malice." Read it slowly. Then ask God honestly: is any of this living in me — not erupting, but sitting quietly, beneath the surface? If he shows you something, do not argue with it. Simply bring it to him, and ask him to do what only he can do.

Bring one honest reflection to the start of Session 5.

elements.question1: It's worth having these passages open as you work through this session.

elements.text1:
Kemi had not seen the church account statement in seven years of marriage.

She did not think of herself as someone who was controlled. Her husband was not cruel or deliberately secretive — he was simply a man who had grown up in a household where money was the husband's business, and who had married a woman who had grown up in the same kind of household. Neither of them had questioned it. It was simply how things were.

But the previous month, when their eldest daughter needed school fees that they did not have and could not discuss together because neither of them had a clear picture of what was actually available, something had shifted in Kemi. She began to ask questions she had not asked before. Not accusatory questions — she was careful about that — but honest ones. What do we actually have? What do we actually owe? What are we actually giving to the church from our personal account versus what the church provides for us? And the largest question, the one she had not quite allowed herself to form until now: Is this sustainable? And is it fair?

She did not yet have answers. But she had begun to ask — and that, in itself, felt like a kind of freedom she had not known she was missing.

This session is about the practical realities of household life in a ministry context: money and financial transparency, children and the specific pressures on pastors' families, the question of whether and how a pastor's wife might work outside the home, and the wider question of freedom — the freedom God intends for the women he has called, in all its practical dimensions.

These are not abstract theological topics. They are Tuesday-morning realities. The Bible speaks into all of them.
Part 1 — Provision and Responsibility

The New Testament takes the material needs of households and communities seriously. It does not spiritualise poverty or romanticise financial difficulty. Paul writes directly and practically about the obligation to care for family members, the responsibility of the church toward its workers, and the principles that should govern how money flows in a Christian community.

The passage in 1 Timothy 5 is about the church's care for widows — but embedded within it is one of the most direct statements in the New Testament about financial responsibility within households. It is stronger than it is usually preached.

elements.passageText1:
3Honor widows who are truly widows. 4But if a widow has children or grandchildren, they should first learn to fulfill their duty toward their own household and so repay their parents what is owed them. For this is what pleases God. 5But the widow who is truly in need, and completely on her own, has set her hope on God and continues in her pleas and prayers night and day. 6But the one who lives for pleasure is dead even while she lives. 7Reinforce these commands, so that they will be beyond reproach. 8But if someone does not provide for his own, especially his own family, he has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever.


(NET — see context ↗)

elements.header1: "If anyone does not provide for his own relatives" (v8)
elements.question1: Paul says that failing to provide for one's own household is a denial of the faith — stronger language than almost anything else he says about practical ethics. The context is the care of widows, but the principle is broad: the Christian household has a non-negotiable responsibility to its members. What does this suggest about the priority of the family's material wellbeing — including the pastor's family — within a ministry context?

deeper.question1: Many church cultures, particularly in contexts of financial pressure, create an implicit expectation that the pastor's family will model self-sacrifice — giving to the church at the expense of their own household needs, living visibly simply as a witness to faith. That impulse is not entirely wrong. But where does it become harmful — and how do you distinguish genuine gospel generosity from exploitation dressed in spiritual language?

elements.question1: The passage distinguishes between a widow who has family to care for her and one who is "truly alone" (v5) — the church's obligation is specifically to those who have no other resource. This is a picture of the church as a safety net for genuine need, not a replacement for family responsibility. What does this principle suggest about how a pastor's family should think about the relationship between their private household finances and their public ministry giving?

elements.passageText1:
13It is not our intention that others should be relieved while you are burdened, but that as a matter of equality 14your abundance at the present time should supply their need, so that their abundance may also supply your need, and thus there may be equality — 15as it is written: ‘The one who gathered much did not have too much, and the one who gathered little did not have too little.’


(NET — see context ↗)

elements.question1: Paul's principle in 2 Corinthians 8 is "equality" — not uniformity, but a genuine balance in which abundance in one place addresses need in another, so that "there may be equality." He quotes the manna story from Exodus 16: those who gathered much did not have too much; those who gathered little did not have too little. What does this principle of sufficiency — enough, but not at the expense of others — say to the question of how a pastor's family handles both abundance and scarcity?

deeper.question1: Paul says the goal is not that "others should be relieved while you are burdened" (v13) — the opposite of the pattern he is correcting, but equally unbalanced. A pastor's wife who gives until her own household is in genuine need is not modelling the gospel. She is modelling a different kind of imbalance. Is there a way in which you have accepted a burden that was not yours to carry — financial, practical, or emotional — out of a sense that it was expected of you in your role? What would it mean to lay it down?

elements.term1: Financial transparency in a ministry marriage
elements.question1: Is it appropriate for a pastor's wife to have full visibility and a genuine voice in the household's financial decisions?
elements.answer1:
Yes — and the absence of financial transparency in a marriage is a serious problem, regardless of the cultural norms that may normalise it.

A marriage is a full partnership. The principle of one flesh (Genesis 2:24) extends to finances as much as to any other dimension of life. A wife who does not know what her household earns, what it owes, what it gives, and what it has available is not a partner — she is a dependent. And dependence without information is a form of vulnerability, not a form of trust.

In a ministry context, financial transparency has additional dimensions: it is important that the pastor's family's personal finances are clearly separated from the church's finances, that the household's income from ministry is understood by both partners, and that decisions about giving — to the church, to family members, to the wider community — are made together rather than unilaterally.

This is not about distrust of a husband. It is about the basic dignity and responsibility that belong to an equal partner in a household. Proverbs 31 describes a woman who "considers a field and buys it" (v16), who manages income from her own trading (v18), who gives generously to the poor (v20). She knows her household's financial position because she helps to shape it.

If your household does not currently operate with financial transparency, beginning that conversation is not a challenge to your husband's authority. It is an invitation to the kind of partnership that the Bible describes and that a healthy marriage requires. Start gently. Start with curiosity rather than accusation. But start.

elements.text1: Part 2 — Women Who Worked

One of the most persistent questions for pastors' wives — in Nigeria and beyond — is whether and how they might work outside the home. The question carries a weight of expectation: some church cultures insist a pastor's wife must be domestically focused; others celebrate her professional achievements as a sign of the ministry's credibility. Both positions can be forms of pressure rather than freedom.

The Bible's witness on women and work is more varied and more liberating than either extreme suggests. Women in Scripture work — outside the home, in business, in service, in leadership — without apology or explanation. And the first followers of Jesus include women of financial means who fund his ministry from their own resources.

elements.passageText1:
1Soon afterward Jesus began going around from city to city and from village to village, proclaiming and telling the good news of the kingdom of God. The twelve were with him, 2and also some women who had been healed of evil spirits and various illnesses: Mary Magdalene, from whom seven demons had gone out, 3Joanna the wife of Chuza, Herod's household manager, and Susanna, and many others. These women were ministering to them out of their own means.


(NET — see context ↗)

elements.header1: "Who provided for them out of their own resources" (v3)
elements.question1: Luke names several women who travelled with Jesus and the twelve and who funded the ministry from their own means — their own resources, their own money. This is presented without comment or justification. What does the matter-of-fact tone of this passage say about women's financial agency and working capacity in the earliest Christian community? And what does it say to the assumption that a pastor's wife should not have an independent income or financial life?

deeper.question1: Joanna, named in this passage, is described in Luke 24:10 as one of the women at the resurrection. She is the wife of Chuza — Herod's household manager — a woman of social standing who has apparently made her own decision to follow Jesus and fund his ministry. Her husband is not mentioned as part of Jesus' group. What does her independent discipleship and financial contribution say about the relationship between a woman's faith, her finances, and her marriage?

elements.passageText1:
10A capable wife — who can find one? She is far more precious than jewels. 11The heart of her husband trusts in her, and he has no lack of gain. 12She does him good and not harm all the days of her life. 13She seeks out wool and flax, and works with willing hands. 14She is like merchant ships; she brings her food from far away. 15She rises while it is still night and provides food for her household and a portion for her female servants. 16She considers a field and buys it; with the fruit of her hands she plants a vineyard. 17She clothes herself with strength and makes her arms strong. 18She perceives that her merchandise is profitable; her lamp does not go out at night. 19She puts her hands to the distaff, and her hands grasp the spindle. 20She is generous to the poor, and extends a helping hand to the needy. 21She is not afraid for her household when it snows, for all her household is clothed in crimson. 22She makes bed coverings for herself; her clothing is fine linen and purple. 23Her husband is well known at the city gate, where he sits with the elders of the land. 24She makes linen garments and sells them; she supplies sashes to the merchants. 25She is clothed with strength and honour, and she laughs at the time to come. 26She opens her mouth with wisdom, and loving instruction is on her tongue. 27She watches over the ways of her household, and does not eat the bread of idleness. 28Her children rise up and call her blessed; her husband also praises her: 29‘Many women have done excellently, but you surpass them all.’ 30Charm is deceitful and beauty is fleeting, but a woman who fears the Lord will be praised. 31Give her the reward she has earned, and let her works praise her at the city gates.


(NET — see context ↗)

elements.question1: We looked at Proverbs 31 in Volume 1, Session 3, in the context of marriage and partnership. Here we return to it through the lens of work and finances. The woman of Proverbs 31 buys fields (v16), plants vineyards (v16), trades profitably (v18), makes and sells linen garments (v24), and gives to the poor (v20). She has an active economic life, external to the household, that is entirely her own. How does this portrait challenge the expectation that a godly wife — and particularly a pastor's wife — should be financially inactive or dependent?

The Proverbs 31 woman's household is well-clothed and well-fed (vv21–22) — her family does not suffer because she is economically active. The assumption that a wife working outside the home necessarily means her household is neglected is not supported by this portrait. What does a well-ordered household that includes a working wife actually require — from the wife, from the husband, and from the structures around them?

deeper.question1: In your specific context — the size of your husband's church, your current season of life, your own gifts and training, your financial situation — what is the question of work outside the home actually about for you? Is it a live question, a settled one, or one you have never felt free to ask? What would a genuinely free and prayerful conversation with your husband about it look like?

elements.term1: "A pastor's wife should not work — it suggests the church cannot support them."
elements.question1: Is it spiritually or reputationally problematic for a pastor's wife to have her own employment or income?
elements.answer1:
This expectation — that a pastor's wife working outside the home reflects poorly on the church or her husband's provision — is cultural in origin, not biblical.

The New Testament itself gives us Priscilla, who worked as a tentmaker alongside her husband Aquila and the apostle Paul (Acts 18:3). Their shared craft was not a sign of spiritual failure — it was simply how they lived and funded their ministry. Paul himself worked with his hands throughout his apostolic ministry, partly as a matter of principle and partly as a matter of necessity (1 Corinthians 9:1–18; 2 Thessalonians 3:7–9).

A pastor's wife who works outside the home is not signalling that the church is struggling. She may be using gifts that God gave her before the ministry existed. She may be contributing to household financial stability in a way that actually protects the ministry from the distorting pressures of financial dependency. She may be building relationships with people outside the church who would never attend a Sunday service. She may simply be doing something she loves and is good at.

What matters is not whether she works, but whether the decision has been made freely — together with her husband, before God — rather than under pressure from cultural expectations in either direction. The woman who works because she feels she must, and the woman who does not work because she feels she must not, are both less free than the woman who has genuinely asked the question and made her own considered choice.

Freedom, not conformity in either direction, is the goal.

elements.text1: Part 3 — Children in the Manse

Children raised in a pastor's household carry a particular set of pressures that are rarely named honestly. They are expected, often without anyone saying so directly, to be better-behaved, more spiritually mature, and more compliant than other children — because their behaviour reflects on their father's ministry. They grow up in a household where the phone rings at all hours, where strangers claim their parents' time and attention, where privacy is limited and public performance is constant.

Some flourish in that environment. Others carry its weight for decades. Many do both.

The Bible does not give a specific theology of the pastor's child. But it gives a rich theology of children — of their dignity, their spiritual capacity, their need for presence rather than performance, and their place in the community of faith. And it gives a searching challenge to parents about what is actually being passed on in the ordinary texture of daily life.

We return briefly here to Deuteronomy 6 — which we first read in Session 2 of this volume — because its vision of intergenerational faith formation has as much to say to parents as it does to disciplers.

elements.passageText1:
4Listen, Israel: The Lord is our God, the Lord alone. 5You must love the Lord your God with your whole mind, your whole being, and all your strength. 6These words I am commanding you today must be kept in mind, 7and you must teach them to your children and speak of them as you sit in your house, as you walk along the road, as you lie down, and as you get up. 8You should tie them as a reminder on your forearm and fasten them as symbols on your forehead. 9Inscribe them on the doorframes of your houses and gates.


(NET — see context ↗)

elements.question1: The unspoken expectation that a pastor's children must be exemplary can put those children in a position where they feel they are performing faith rather than discovering it for themselves. What is the difference between a child who is genuinely forming their own faith and a child who is performing the faith expected of them? And how do you create the conditions for the former rather than the latter in your own household?

deeper.question1: Many adult children of pastors describe feeling that the congregation came before them — that their parents were more available to church members than to their own family. Is that a tension you recognise in your household? What specific, practical things do you do — or could you do — to protect your children from feeling that they are secondary to the ministry?

elements.question1: Deuteronomy 6:7 calls parents to talk about God's words "when you sit in your house, and when you walk by the way, and when you lie down, and when you rise." This is faith formation in the grain of ordinary time — not in the church programme, but in the household. For a pastor's family, whose religious life is publicly extensive, what is the quality of faith formation in the private, ordinary, daily texture of home? Is there a gap between the public and the private?

Your children watch how you handle conflict, rest, disappointment, money, and criticism — they are forming their understanding of what the Christian life looks like from the inside of your household. What do you think they are currently learning from what they observe? Is there anything you would want to be different?

deeper.question1: Some pastors' children grow up to have a deep and genuine faith; others walk away from the church entirely. Research consistently suggests that the quality of the parent-child relationship — warmth, honesty, genuine presence — matters more than the quantity of religious activity in the home. What does that finding say to the way you are currently investing in your relationship with your children, as distinct from their religious formation?

elements.text1: Part 4 — Contentment and Freedom

Paul writes to the Philippians from prison. He is not writing about financial management from a position of comfortable sufficiency — he is writing from a cell, uncertain of his future, deeply grateful for the Philippians' gift, and yet able to say something remarkable: he has learned to be content in all circumstances. Not that contentment came naturally. Not that circumstances do not matter. He has learned it — which means it took time, practice, and the specific discipline of having been through both abundance and need.

His contentment is not resignation. It is a kind of freedom — a freedom from the tyranny of circumstances, rooted in something that circumstances cannot touch.

This is the note on which this session ends: not with a resolution of every financial and practical question, but with the deeper freedom that the gospel offers — a freedom that can hold difficult circumstances without being defined by them.

elements.passageText1:
10I have great joy in the Lord because now at last you have again expressed your concern for me. (Now I know you were concerned before, but had no opportunity to show it.) 11I am not saying this because I am in need, for I have learned to be content in any circumstance. 12I have experienced times of need and times of abundance. In any and every circumstance I have learned the secret of contentment, whether I go satisfied or hungry, have plenty or nothing. 13I am able to do all things through the one who strengthens me.


(NET — see context ↗)

elements.header1: "I have learned, in whatever situation I am, to be content" (v11)
elements.question1: Paul says contentment is something he has learned — not a natural disposition, not a spiritual gift, but something acquired through experience. He has been through abundance and need, fullness and hunger. What do you think the process of learning contentment actually involves? What experiences, and what disciplines, form a person who can hold both abundance and scarcity without being destabilised by either?

deeper.question1: "I am able to do all things through the one who strengthens me" (v13) is one of the most quoted verses in the New Testament — and one of the most frequently misapplied, used as a declaration of general capability rather than what Paul actually means: a statement about his capacity to endure both plenty and want without losing his footing. In its actual context, what is this verse saying — and how does that change what it means to you in your specific circumstances right now?

elements.question1: Paul distinguishes between contentment and passivity. He is grateful for the Philippians' gift. He is glad his situation has improved. He is not pretending that material circumstances are irrelevant. Contentment is not the same as accepting injustice or suppressing legitimate need. What is the difference between a contentment that is genuine freedom and a contentment that is simply a spiritual word for giving up on things that ought to be different?

Kemi, in our opening story, began to ask questions she had not asked before — about money, about fairness, about sustainability. That asking felt like a kind of freedom. What questions about your own household — financial, practical, relational — have you not yet felt free to ask? And what has made them feel unsafe or impermissible?

deeper.question1: Freedom, in Paul's vision, is not the absence of constraint — he is writing from prison. It is an inner settledness that holds whatever circumstances bring. At the same time, he receives help, asks for what he needs, and is genuinely glad when his situation improves. How do you hold together the call to contentment and the legitimate pursuit of change — in your finances, in your household, in your ministry arrangements — without collapsing one into the other?

elements.question1: How do you begin a conversation about financial transparency and household finances if it has never been the pattern in your marriage?
elements.answer1:
Carefully, honestly, and without accusation — but do begin.

The goal of the conversation is not to establish control or to challenge your husband's integrity. It is to move toward the kind of genuine partnership that a healthy marriage requires, and that the Bible describes. Frame it that way.

Some starting points:

Start with a shared question rather than a complaint. ‘I'd like us to have a clearer picture together of where we are financially — can we set a time to go through it?’ is very different from ‘I never know what money we have.’ The first invites partnership; the second provokes defensiveness.

Separate the household budget from the church finances clearly. If this boundary is currently blurred — if it is unclear what the church provides, what the household earns independently, and what personal money goes to the church — that clarity is the first and most important thing to establish. It protects your husband as much as it protects you.

Name what you need to know, and why. You need to know the household's financial position so that you can make good decisions, care well for your children, give generously from a place of genuine knowledge, and avoid the kind of uninformed vulnerability that Kemi experienced. These are good reasons. State them plainly.

Be patient with the process. If your household has operated a certain way for years, changing that pattern takes time and requires trust to be built on both sides. One honest conversation is a beginning, not an arrival.

If the conversation meets with resistance — if your husband is unwilling to share financial information with you at all — that is a more serious issue that may warrant support from a trusted pastor, counsellor, or mentor couple. Financial control is a recognised form of harm in marriage, and you do not have to accept it as normal.

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements1: I have a clear and honest picture of my household's financial situation.
I feel that financial decisions in my household are made together, with my full participation.
I feel free to consider working outside the home, or to continue doing so, without guilt or external pressure.
I am intentional about protecting my children from the specific pressures of ministry life.
I have a genuine, learned contentment that can hold both abundance and difficulty without losing its footing.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Kemi's freedom began when she started asking questions she had not asked before. Is there a question — about money, about work, about your children, about the practical shape of your household — that you have been avoiding? What would it take to ask it?

Paul learned contentment through experience — through the actual living of abundance and need. Looking back at your own financial and practical history in ministry, what have you learned that you could not have learned any other way? Is there a contentment you have arrived at that was not there at the beginning?

Of the three areas this session has covered — finances and provision, work and economic life, children and household formation — which one is most live for you right now? What is one honest, specific, loving conversation you need to have — with your husband, with God, or with yourself — about it?

elements.text1:
The practical realities of household life in ministry are not a distraction from the spiritual life. They are part of it. The way money flows in your household is a spiritual matter. The way your children experience home is a spiritual matter. The way you think about your own working life and financial agency is a spiritual matter. The gospel speaks into all of it — not with a single prescribed pattern, but with principles of partnership, dignity, sufficiency, and freedom that are more demanding and more liberating than any cultural template.

Kemi began to find freedom when she started asking questions. The questions were not a sign of disloyalty or distrust. They were a sign that she was beginning to take seriously her place as a full partner in her own household — which is exactly what God designed her to be.

You are not a dependent. You are not a passenger. You are not a supporting character in someone else's story.

You are a partner. An equal heir. A woman with gifts, responsibilities, and a voice.

Use it.

elements.question1: What is your challenge for this week?
elements.answer1:
Three things — choose the one or two that are most relevant to where you are.

On finances: This week, initiate one conversation with your husband about your household's financial picture. Keep it simple and non-confrontational — perhaps just: ‘Can we find an hour this month to look at our finances together? I'd like us both to have a clearer picture.’ Then follow through when he says yes.

On work: If the question of whether and how you might work outside the home is live for you — or has been live and unresolved for a long time — set aside thirty minutes this week to write honestly about it. Not a decision, just an honest account of where you are: what you want, what you feel free to want, what feels constrained, and what you sense God might be saying. Then, if it feels right, share some of it with your husband.

On your children: Choose one specific thing this week that is just for your children — not a church event, not a ministry commitment, not something that happens to include them. Something chosen for them, because they are yours and they matter. Afterwards, notice what it costs you — in time, in margin — and whether it was worth it.

Bring one reflection from whichever of these you chose to the start of Session 6 — our final session.

elements.question1: It’s worth having these passages open as you work through this session.

elements.text1:
Mama Bello turned seventy-three the week before the conference.

The women who had gathered — pastors’ wives from across three states, some of them decades younger than her — had not expected her to say what she said. They had expected wisdom. They had expected encouragement. They had expected perhaps a testimony of God’s faithfulness across fifty years of ministry life. They got all of that.

But they had not expected the honesty.

She told them about the years when the church plant had nearly collapsed — when there were eleven people in the room and her husband had wept in the car on the way home. She told them about the miscarriage no one in the congregation had known about, and the months afterwards when she had been angry at God in a way she had never previously admitted to anyone outside her marriage. She told them about the deacon who had tried to remove her husband from the church and the eighteen months of warfare that followed. She told them about the season, in her early sixties, when she had felt she had nothing left to give — and how God had surprised her in that emptiness with a calling she had not anticipated: the kitchen-table discipleship that had become, in her last decade, the most fruitful thing she had ever done.

She told them, with the directness of someone who had run out of reasons to be careful, that ministry is long and that the middle is harder than the beginning and that finishing well is not about arriving somewhere impressive. It is about still being in love with Jesus when you get there.

"The women at my table," she said — she always called her disciples "her women" — "they are my finishing well. They are my 2 Timothy 2:2. The rest," she paused, and smiled, "the rest was just getting here."

This is the final session of a two-volume study. It is about the long view — finishing well, passing on what has been given to you, and understanding that the most significant fruit of a ministry life is often not what you planned, not what was visible, and not what you will live to see fully grown.
Part 1 — The Race That Is Already Won

Paul writes 2 Timothy from prison, expecting execution. He is not writing from a position of arrival — from the summit of a successful life, looking back with satisfaction at a job well done. He is writing from a cell, at the end of a road that included beatings, shipwrecks, betrayal by close associates, and sustained misunderstanding. He has been through what most of us will never face.

And yet the tone of these verses is not bitter, not resigned, and not performing a courage he does not feel. It is something quieter and more durable than triumph: a settled confidence that the race was worth running, that the faith was worth keeping, and that what is ahead is secure — not because of what he has achieved, but because of who he has known.

elements.header1: "I have finished the race" (v7)
elements.question1: Paul uses three images: a drink offering being poured out, a finished race, a kept faith. All three describe completion — not abandonment, not collapse, not a stumbling finish, but the actual crossing of the line. Finishing well, in Paul’s framing, is not about ending impressively. It is about still being in the race when it ends. What do you think the greatest threats to finishing well are — for a pastor’s wife specifically — over the long course of a ministry life?

deeper.question1: Paul says "I have kept the faith" — not "I have kept my reputation" or "I have kept my position" or "I have kept the ministry going." The faith is the thing. In a long ministry life, it is possible to maintain everything external — the role, the attendance, the appearance of fruitfulness — while the interior faith quietly drains away. What are the signs, in your own life, that the faith is being kept — and what are the warning signs that it might be leaking?

elements.question1: Verse 8 speaks of a "crown of righteousness" laid up for Paul — and then adds: "not only to me, but also to all who have loved his appearing." The reward is not for the most prominent, the most productive, or the most visibly successful. It is for all who have loved Christ’s appearing — which includes the woman who discipled three people at her kitchen table across thirty years and was never on a conference platform. What does that inclusion say about how God measures a ministry life?

Paul has clearly made peace with his situation — including the fact that some people have deserted him (v10, 16), that his first defence was attended by no one (v16), and that the end is near. This is not denial or false positivity. It is a real and costly peace. What do you think produces that kind of peace — not at the end of life, but in the ordinary middle of ministry, in the seasons when things are hard and the outcome is uncertain?

deeper.question1: "The Lord stood by me and strengthened me" (v17) — Paul’s confidence in the difficult season is not in his own resilience but in a presence. Looking back at your own ministry life so far, can you identify moments when the Lord stood by you and strengthened you — moments you might have named differently at the time, but which you can now see more clearly? What do those moments tell you about what the next stretch of the road will hold?

elements.text1: Part 2 — When You Stay

Ruth is one of the most compressed and luminous narratives in the Old Testament. In the space of four chapters it covers bereavement, displacement, loyalty, poverty, hard work, unexpected grace, and restoration. But at its hinge — at the moment on which everything else turns — is a single act of choice that is so quiet it is easy to underestimate.

Naomi tells her daughters-in-law to go back to their mothers’ houses. She has nothing to offer them. Her husband is dead, her sons are dead, she is going back to a country they do not know, to a God who — by her own account — has dealt bitterly with her. She is, from every practical angle, a terrible investment.

And Ruth stays.

She stays not because staying is sensible, not because she has calculated the odds and found them acceptable, but because she has made a commitment that she does not intend to unmake simply because circumstances have become difficult. Her speech to Naomi is one of the great declarations of loyalty in all of literature — and it contains within it a theology of long faithfulness that speaks directly to the question of what it means to stay in ministry when everything argues for leaving.

elements.header1: "Where you go, I will go" (v16)
elements.question1: Ruth’s commitment to Naomi is total and unconditional — she names it across every dimension of life: where you go, where you lodge, your people, your God, where you die, where you are buried. This is not a temporary arrangement subject to review if circumstances improve. It is a permanent orientation of the self toward another person’s good. What does that kind of commitment look like, in practice, in a long ministry life — toward God, toward your husband, toward the community you serve?

deeper.question1: Ruth makes her commitment when Naomi has nothing to offer — when the investment looks terrible on every measurable axis. Orpah, who also loved Naomi, nonetheless goes back (v14) — and the text does not condemn her for it. It was the sensible choice. But Ruth’s choice is the one the whole story turns on. Is there a commitment in your own life — to a marriage, to a community, to a calling — that you have stayed in when leaving would have been understandable? What kept you? And what do you think that staying has produced that you could not have anticipated?

elements.question1: Naomi, at this point in the story, is not an easy person to be with. She is bitter (v20), she feels abandoned by God, and she is not pretending otherwise. Ruth commits to her anyway — to her bitterness as well as her need. What does it mean to stay with someone who is in a difficult season, when they have little to give and the relationship costs more than it returns? And what does Ruth’s choice say to the question of faithfulness in relationships that go through seasons of asymmetry?

Ruth does not know, at the moment of her choice, that the story ends in Boaz and restoration and a son who will be in the lineage of David and, through him, of Jesus. She makes her choice in the dark, without knowing the outcome. The fruit of her faithfulness is visible only from a distance she cannot see. What does that hiddenness — that gap between faithful choice and visible fruit — say about how God works in the long course of a faithful life?

deeper.question1: Ruth 4:17 records that the women of the neighbourhood named Ruth’s son Obed — and that Obed was the father of Jesse, the father of David. Ruth’s faithfulness in one small, unremarkable choice on a road in Moab is woven into the ancestry of the Messiah. She could not have known that. We rarely know the full significance of our own faithful choices either. Is there a small, unglamorous faithfulness in your own life — something you have been doing consistently, without recognition, without visible fruit — that you need to trust to God’s larger purposes?

elements.question1: What does finishing well actually look like for a pastor’s wife?
elements.answer1:
It does not look like arriving at a large platform, a well-known name, or a ministry résumé that impresses a conference audience.

Paul, writing from a prison cell with most of his companions gone, calls it finished well. Ruth, gleaning in a field as a foreign widow with nothing to her name, is one of the great heroes of the faith. Lois and Eunice, whose names appear once in a single verse of a letter most people have not read carefully, shaped the faith of someone who shaped the church of every century since.

Finishing well means still loving Jesus when you get there. It means having kept the faith — not kept the role, not kept the reputation, not kept the programme running, but kept the actual living trust in the actual living God. It means having passed on what was given to you, to at least one other person, who will pass it on in turn. It means having been genuinely present — to your husband, to your children, to the women God placed in your path — even when presence was costly and invisibility would have been easier.

It means being able to say, as Paul did, not "look what I built" but "I have known him. And it was worth it."

That is available to every pastor’s wife, in every size of church, in every season of life. It does not require a platform. It requires faithfulness — the long, ordinary, costly, beautiful kind.

elements.text1: Part 3 — Still Becoming

One of the quiet dangers of a long ministry life is the assumption that formation is something that happens at the beginning — in the early years of faith, in the formative seasons of marriage and calling — and that what follows is simply the application of what has already been learned.

Paul, writing to the Philippians from prison — late in his apostolic life, after decades of ministry — describes himself as someone still in the process of becoming. He has not arrived. He is still being formed. He is still reaching forward. And the image he uses is one of the most energetic in all his letters: a runner, straining, pressing on.

Caleb, in Joshua 14, makes one of the most arresting claims in the Old Testament — at the age of eighty-five, standing before Joshua and asking for the hardest territory still to be taken. His argument is simple: God is still faithful, I am still capable, and there is still work to be done.

Neither of them is coasting. Neither of them has settled.

elements.header1: "Forgetting what lies behind and straining forward to what lies ahead" (v13)
elements.question1: Paul says he forgets what lies behind — and this from the man who has just spent three verses describing his impressive religious credentials (vv4–6) and then calling them rubbish (v8). The things behind him include both achievements and failures. Both need to be released. What do you need to forget — or release — from your own past in ministry, in order to run the next stretch of the race unencumbered? Is it a failure, a wound, a disappointment, or an achievement that has become a resting place?

deeper.question1: "Straining forward" (epekteinomenos in Greek) is an image of physical effort — a runner leaning into the finish line, every muscle extended toward what is ahead. Paul is not describing a relaxed amble toward the end of life. He is describing active, effortful engagement with what is still to come. Whatever season of ministry you are in — early, middle, or later — what is the "what lies ahead" that you are currently straining toward? What is the next thing God has placed before you, and what would it mean to reach for it with everything you have?

elements.question1: Caleb is eighty-five years old and asks for the mountain — the hardest, most contested territory still to be taken. His argument is that he is as strong now as he was at forty (v11), that God has kept him (v10), and that with God’s help he will drive out the enemies that remain (v12). What is the theological foundation of Caleb’s confidence — and what does his refusal to settle for the easier territory say about the call to faithful engagement at every stage of life?

deeper.question1: Caleb had been waiting forty-five years for this moment — forty-five years since Moses had promised him this territory (v9), forty years of wilderness wandering that were not his fault, and five more years of other people’s battles. He waited without bitterness and arrived ready. Is there something you have been waiting for — a calling deferred, a season delayed, a promise not yet fulfilled — that Caleb’s story speaks into? What does it mean to arrive at the deferred thing still ready, still expectant, still asking for the mountain?

elements.question1: Paul says we are being transformed "from glory to glory" — a progressive, continuous, ongoing process that does not conclude until we see him face to face. Transformation into Christ’s image is not something that happened at conversion and is now complete. It is the work of a lifetime, carried out by the Spirit as we behold him. What does it mean to you — practically, personally — that you are still being formed, still becoming, still being transformed? What is the Spirit currently working on in you?

elements.text1: Part 4 — What You Leave Behind

Every ministry life leaves something behind. The question is not whether you will leave a legacy, but what kind. Not every legacy is visible. Not every significant investment produces fruit that the investor lives to see. Not every Lois and Eunice knows there is a Timothy.

But the investments are real. The formation is real. The faith passed on is real. And the God who sees in secret — who we met in Session 4 of Volume 1 — is the one who accounts for all of it, including the things done in the kitchen and at the bedside and on the step and over the phone in the middle of the night when no one was watching.

This final section is not about achieving a legacy. It is about living faithfully enough that a legacy becomes possible — because you loved the right things, in the right order, for long enough.

elements.question1: Mama Bello, in our opening story, said her women were her "finishing well" — her 2 Timothy 2:2. The most significant fruit of her fifty years of ministry was not the events she organised or the programmes she ran. It was the women she sat with at her kitchen table. Looking ahead — five years, ten years, twenty — what do you hope the significant fruit of your own ministry life will be? Not what you hope to have achieved, but what you hope will still be growing when you are gone?

deeper.question1: Ruth’s act of faithfulness on a road in Moab is woven into the lineage of Jesus. Lois and Eunice’s investment in Timothy shaped the church of twenty centuries. Neither of them could have known the full significance of what they were doing. You will probably not know the full significance of what you are doing either. How do you remain faithful in small, unglamorous, unrewarded investment when you cannot see where it leads? What sustains that kind of faithfulness over the long haul?

elements.question1: We have met many women across these twelve sessions — Ngozi, Adaeze, Blessing, Chisom, Amina, Funke, Yetunde, Grace, Chiamaka, Hauwa, Taiwo, Kemi, and Mama Bello. Each of them is a composite sketch of real women in real ministry situations. Which of them has stayed with you most? And what does the one that stayed with you most tell you about where you are in your own story right now?

These two volumes began with a question: Who are you when you are not being useful? Twelve sessions later, how would you answer that question now — not as a theological statement, but as a personal one, from the inside of your own life?

deeper.question1: Mama Bello said the rest — all of it, the fifty years of ministry — was "just getting here." Here, to the kitchen table. Here, to the women. Here, to the thing that turned out to matter most. As you come to the end of this study, what has this been "getting you here" to? What has shifted, clarified, or begun to take shape that was not there when you started?

elements.term1: The grace available at every stage
elements.question1: What if you are reading this at a point where you feel you have already lost significant ground — in faith, in marriage, in the hidden life — and finishing well feels like a description of someone else’s story?
elements.answer1:
Then this note is for you.

The gospel is not only for people who are starting well. It is for people who are in the middle of a mess, who have made serious mistakes, whose marriages are strained, whose faith has gone cold, whose hidden life is emptier than they have admitted to anyone. It is for people who looked at these twelve sessions and felt, more than anything else, the distance between where they are and where they are supposed to be.

Grace does not begin when you have sorted yourself out. It begins now, exactly where you are, with exactly what you have. Paul did not write "I have kept the faith" from a position of never having failed — he had persecuted the church, he had opposed the gospel, he had watched Stephen die. He wrote it from the far side of an encounter with Jesus that changed everything, followed by decades of costly, imperfect, grace-sustained faithfulness.

Caleb did not arrive at eighty-five unscarred. He had walked forty years in a wilderness that was not his fault, surrounded by people who had given up. He arrived still ready because the God who had been faithful for forty-five years was still faithful now.

You are not too far gone. You are not too late. You are not too broken for the rest of the story to be good.

The grace available at the beginning is exactly the same grace available now. And the one who started the work in you is, as Paul says to the Philippians, still committed to bringing it to completion — "until the day of Christ Jesus" (Philippians 1:6).

That day has not yet come. Which means the work is not finished. Which means there is still time.

Begin again. Begin now.

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement — looking at the full arc of these two volumes.
statements1: I have a clearer sense of my identity in Christ than I did when I began this study.
I feel more equipped to navigate the specific challenges of ministry life.
There is at least one significant thing that has shifted in how I think about myself, my marriage, or my calling.
I am investing — or more ready to invest — in at least one other woman.
I have a genuine hope about the next season of my ministry life.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Mama Bello’s most honest moment — telling the story of the miscarriage, the anger at God, the warfare with the deacon — was also her most useful one. The women in the room needed to hear the real story, not the polished version. As you come to the end of this study, what is the real story of your ministry life so far — the honest version, not the testimony meeting version? And who needs to hear some of it from you?

Paul forgot what was behind and strained toward what was ahead. Caleb asked for the mountain. Ruth stayed on the road. What is the posture you most need to carry into the next season of your ministry life — releasing something, pressing toward something, or simply staying faithful to something already begun?

At the end of these twelve sessions — what is the one thing you most want to carry forward? Not a resolution or a programme, but a truth, a posture, a practice, or a person. Name it as specifically as you can.

elements.text1:
You began this study with a question: Who are you when you are not being useful?

The answer the Bible has given, across twelve sessions, is this:

You are a daughter of God, chosen before the world was made. You are known completely and loved without condition — not because of what you do, but because of who he is. You are a partner in your marriage, not an assistant. You are a jar of clay carrying extraordinary treasure, and your fragility is part of how the light gets out. You are permitted to lament, to struggle, to run dry — and to be met there by a God who feeds before he sends. You are a branch, not a trunk, and your fruitfulness comes from remaining in him. You have gifts that are yours — given to you, not borrowed from your husband’s calling. You have something to pass on that no one else can pass on in quite the same way. You have a pastoral heart, or the beginning of one. You are navigating conflict with more wisdom than you had before. You are learning, however slowly, to ask the questions you were afraid to ask. And you are, still, becoming — transformed from glory to glory, by the Spirit of the Lord, into something you have not yet fully seen.

The ministry is long. The middle is harder than the beginning. The fruit is often hidden and the cost is sometimes higher than you expected.

But the one who called you is faithful. The race is worth running. The faith is worth keeping.

And the women at your table — whoever they are, however few, however quiet the kitchen — they are your finishing well.

Run toward it.

elements.question1: The study is ending. What comes next?
elements.answer1:
Three things — and you may not be ready for all of them yet. That is all right.

Keep one practice from this study. Not all of them — one. The practice that has made the most difference, or that you have not yet fully tried. Protect it. Give it enough time to become a rhythm rather than an experiment. Whether it is the regular lament, the unhurried time at Jesus’ feet, the honest conversation about finances, or the consistent investment in one other woman — keep it, and see what it grows into.

Tell someone what happened here. Not a summary of the content, but a piece of your real story — something that shifted, something that was named, something you had not said before. Find one person who needs to hear it. It does not need to be long or polished. It just needs to be true.

Begin again, if you need to. These twelve sessions will still be here. The women who need them are around you. If there is a woman in your church, your community, or your family who would benefit from walking through this study — with a group, with a friend, alone — you know where it begins.

In Christ, I am…

Finish the sentence. You know the answer now. Go and live it.
