sections.heading2: Don Me Aka Yi Wannan Nazarin?

blocks.title1: Pastors’ wives finding themselves lost in the role
blocks.title2: Matan pastoci da suka tsinci kansu a matsayin da ya mamaye su
blocks.title3: Rewɓe pastor’en majjuɓe nder darajaaji maɓɓe
blocks.body1: If ministry life has slowly swallowed your sense of who you are — if you feel more like an extension of your husband’s work than a person with your own name before God — this study is for you. It works through what the Bible says about identity, character, and calling, not as a pastor’s wife, but as a woman known and loved by Christ.
blocks.body2: Idan rayuwar hidima ta sannu-sannu ta mamaye sanin ko kai wane ne — idan kana jin kamar kai wani ɓangare ne kawai na aikin mijinka maimakon mutum mai sunansa na kansa a gaban Allah — wannan nazarin naka ne. Yana duba abin da Littafi Mai Tsarki ya faɗa game da asali, hali, da kira, ba a matsayin matar pastor ba, amma a matsayin macen da Almasihu ya sani kuma yake ƙauna.
blocks.body3: To ngendam kuugal Allah ɗon ɗon moɗi annditugol hoore ma — to a ɗon nana bana a ɓeydaari dow kuugal gorko ma on, naa neɗɗo mariɗo innde mum yeeso Allah — janngirde nde woni ngam ma. Ɗum ɗon wolwa dow ko Linjiila wi’i dow annditugol hoore, hali, e noddaandu, naa bana debbo pastor, amma bana debbo mo Almasiihu anndi e yiɗi.

blocks.title1: Women carrying the weight of performance
blocks.title2: Mata masu ɗauke da nauyin nuna bajinta
blocks.body1: If you feel the constant pressure to be an example, to hold everything together, and to never be seen struggling, this study gives you permission to set that weight down. It covers grace for yourself, the hidden life that God sees, and the kind of rest that is not laziness but wisdom.
blocks.body2: Idan kana jin matsin lamba akai-akai na zama misali, da kiyaye komai ya tafi daidai, kuma ba a taɓa ganinka kana shan wahala ba, wannan nazarin yana ba ka izinin ajiye wannan nauyin. Ya ƙunshi alheri gare ka, rayuwar ɓoye da Allah yake gani, da kuma irin hutun da ba rago ba ne amma hikima ce.
blocks.body3: To a ɗon nana teddeendi wakkati fu ngam laataago misalu, ngam tiitugo kooɗume, e taa a yi’e a ɗon lanyi, janngirde nde ɗon hokka ma yerduye ngam njippingo teddeendi ndun. Ɗum ɗon wolwa dow mo’ere ngam hoore ma, ngendam lornuɗam ɗam Allah yi’ata, e irin siwtare nde wonaa hulyere amma hikma on.

blocks.title1: Women in ministry at any stage
blocks.title2: Mata a kowane mataki na hidima
blocks.title3: Rewɓe wonɓe nder kuugal Allah koo haa ɗume daraja
blocks.body1: Whether you are newly married to a pastor and still finding your footing, or you have been in ministry for decades and are quietly running on empty, this study meets you where you are. It does not assume a particular role or model of ministry life — it assumes only that you are a woman who wants to know Christ more deeply and live from that centre.
blocks.body2: Ko kai sabuwar matar pastor ce da har yanzu kake ƙoƙarin daidaitawa, ko kuma ka kwashe shekaru da yawa kana hidima kuma kana jin kamar ka gaji sosai a asirce, wannan nazarin zai tarye ka a inda kake. Ba ya ɗauka cewa kana da wani takamaiman matsayi ko salon rayuwar hidima — yana ɗauka ne kawai cewa kai mace ce da take son sanin Almasihu sosai kuma ta rayu bisa wannan tushen.
blocks.body3: Koo a debbo pastor keso mo ɗon ɗabbita laawol, koo a ɗon nder kuugal Allah duuɓi ɗuɗɗi nden a ɗon lanyi bee sembe ma timmuɗum haa asiri, janngirde nde ɗon tawa ma haa ɗon woni. Ɗum tammaaki a mari daraja feere koo irin ngendam kuugal Allah — ɗum tammata tan a debbo jiɗɗo anndugo Almasiihu sosai e wuurdugo dow maajum.

blocks.title3: Kawtal rewɓe wonɓe nder kuugal Allah
blocks.body1: This study works especially well when pastors’ wives work through it together — whether in a local church setting, a denominational gathering, or an informal group of friends. The questions are designed to prompt honest conversation, not polished answers, and the shared experience of ministry life gives the discussions unusual depth.
blocks.body2: Wannan nazarin yana aiki sosai musamman lokacin da matan pastoci suka yi shi tare — ko a cikin cocin gida, taron ƙungiyar darika, ko rukunin abokai na yau da kullun. An tsara tambayoyin ne don motsa tattaunawa ta gaskiya, ba amsoshi na nuna kwarewa ba, kuma kwarewar rayuwar hidima da aka raba tana ba wa tattaunawar zurfin da ba a saba gani ba.
blocks.body3: Janngirde nde ɗon nafa sanne to rewɓe pastor’en ɗon waɗa ɗum kawtal — koo nder kawtal eklessiya, kawtal darika, koo kawtal sooba’en tan. Ƴameeje ɗee njiɗi ummugo haala goonga, naa jaabaawol nuna kawaree, e anndal ngendam kuugal Allah ngal on ngondi nder maajum ɗon hokka tattaunawa ndun miijo lugguɗo.

blocks.body1: This study works well as a personal study. Work through each chapter at your own pace, read the Bible passages, write your answers to the questions, and sit with the reflection questions at the end. Some chapters may move quickly; others may stop you in your tracks. Both are good.
blocks.body2: Wannan nazarin yana aiki da kyau a matsayin nazarin kai. Yi nazarin kowane babi a kan lokacinka, karanta nassosin Littafi Mai Tsarki, rubuta amsoshinka ga tambayoyin, sannan ka yi tunani a kan tambayoyin da ke ƙarshe. Wasu babobi na iya tafiya da sauri; wasu kuma na iya sa ka tsaya ka yi tunani sosai. Dukansu suna da kyau.
blocks.body3: Janngirde nde ɗon nafa bana janngirde hoore neɗɗo. Waɗu kooɗume babi dow sa’a ma, janngu nassiije Linjiila, winndu jaabaawol ma to ƴameeje, nden a numta dow ƴameeje miijitugo ɗe ragare. Babiji feere ɗon yaha law; feere boo ɗon waɗa a daro ngam numtol. Fuu ɗon booɗi.

blocks.body1: The study is designed to be used with other women in ministry, and group discussion brings it alive in a particular way. You could work through each chapter together, or — often more rewarding — each woman works through the chapter individually first, writes her answers, and then the group comes together to discuss what they found. The questions are designed to create honest conversation, not performance.
blocks.body2: An tsara nazarin ne don a yi amfani da shi tare da sauran mata masu hidima, kuma tattaunawar rukuni tana sa shi ya fi tasiri. Kuna iya nazarin kowane babi tare, ko kuma — wanda ya fi ba da fa’ida — kowace mace ta yi nazarin babin ita kaɗai da farko, ta rubuta amsoshinta, sannan rukunin su taru don tattauna abin da suka gano. An tsara tambayoyin ne don samar da tattaunawa ta gaskiya, ba don nuna bajinta ba.
blocks.body3: Ngal janngirde winndaa ngam huutorde bee rewɓe woɓɓe wonɓe nder kuugal Allah, nden tattaunawa kawtal ɗon hokka ɗum mawnunki feere. On waɗan janngirde kooɗume babi kawtal, koo — ko ɓuri nafa — kooɗume debbo waɗa babi on feere mum aran, o winnda jaabaawol makko, nden kawtal ngal fota ngam tattaunago ko ɓe keɓi. Ƴameeje ɗee njiɗi tigguugo tattaunawa goonga, naa nuna bajinta.

blocks.title1: Working with a facilitator or mentor
blocks.title2: Yin aiki tare da mai jagoranci ko mai ba da shawara
blocks.title3: Huutugo bee ekkitinoowo koo neɗɗo mo a hooli
blocks.body1:
It can make a real difference to have someone guiding the group — a more experienced woman in ministry, a counsellor, or a trusted pastor who can create space for honest reflection and help the group think through what they are discovering.

At the end of each chapter, the Share button lets you send your written answers directly to your facilitator or a mentor — via WhatsApp, email, or any other app on your phone. This gives them a window into your thinking before you meet, and makes for much richer conversation.
blocks.body2:
Zai iya kawo babban canji samun wani da yake jagorancin rukunin — macen da ta fi kwarewa a hidima, mai ba da shawara, ko pastor amintacce wanda zai iya samar da sarari don tunani na gaskiya kuma ya taimaka wa rukunin yin tunani a kan abin da suke gano.

A ƙarshen kowane babi, maballin Raba yana ba ka damar aika amsoshinka kai tsaye ga mai jagorancinka ko mai ba ka shawara — ta WhatsApp, imel, ko kowace manhaja a wayarka. Wannan yana ba su damar ganin tunaninka kafin ku haɗu, kuma yana sa tattaunawar ta kasance mai fa’ida sosai.
blocks.body3:
Ɗum ɗon waɗa canji to neɗɗo ɗon ardina kawtal ngal — debbo mariɗo kawaree nder kuugal Allah, ekkitinoowo, koo pastor mo hoolaaɗo mo waɗata nokkuure ngam numtol goonga nden o walla kawtal ngal miijitugo ko ɓe keɓata.

Haa ragare kooɗume babi, buton Yeeyu ɗon hokka ma baawɗe neldugo jaabaawol ma to ekkitinoowo ma koo neɗɗo mo a hooli — bee WhatsApp, email, koo kooɗume manhaja nder waya ma. Ɗum ɗon hokka ɓe laawol yi’ugo numtol ma bako on pota, nden ɗum waɗan tattaunawa ndun mawnugo sanne.

blocks.body1: Each chapter has two kinds of questions. The Bible questions are tied to specific passages — asking you to look carefully at what the text says. The reflection questions at the end are more personal — asking what the chapter means for you, your marriage, and your life in ministry.
blocks.body2: Kowane babi yana da ire-iren tambayoyi biyu. Tambayoyin Littafi Mai Tsarki suna da alaƙa da takamaiman nassosi — suna neman ka duba da kyau abin da nassin ya faɗa. Tambayoyin tunani a ƙarshe sun fi zama na sirri — suna tambayar abin da babin yake nufi a gare ka, aurenka, da rayuwarka a cikin hidima.
blocks.body3: Kooɗume babi ɗon mari irin ƴameeje ɗiɗi. Ƴameeje Linjiila ɗon njiidal bee nassiije feere — ɗon ƴama ma a ndara sosai ko nassi on wi’i. Ƴameeje miijitugo ɗe ragare ɗon ɓuri laataago dow hoore neɗɗo — ɗon ƴama ko babi on fassirta ngam ma, teegal ma, e ngendam ma nder kuugal Allah.

blocks.title1: There are no right answers to the reflection questions
blocks.title2: Babu amsoshi daidai ga tambayoyin tunani
blocks.title3: Walaa jaabaawol goongawol to ƴameeje miijitugo
blocks.body1: The reflection questions are not a test. They are an invitation to honest thought. In a group setting, the most valuable discussions often come not from the woman with the most articulate answer, but from the one who says: I have never thought about this before, or: this one is hard for me. That kind of honesty is what this study is for.
blocks.body2: Tambayoyin tunani ba jarabawa ba ne. Gayyata ce zuwa ga tunani na gaskiya. A cikin rukuni, tattaunawa mafi mahimmanci sau da yawa ba ta fitowa daga macen da ta fi iya amsa tambayoyi ba, amma daga wadda take cewa: Ban taɓa tunani game da wannan ba a baya, ko: wannan yana da wuya a gare ni. Irin wannan gaskiyar ita ce makasudin wannan nazarin.
blocks.body3: Ƴameeje miijitugo naa jarabaaki on. Ɗum noddaandu to numtol goonga on. Nder kawtal, tattaunawa ɓurɗo nafa ɗon urta naa diga debbo ɓurɗo kawaree jaabaawol, amma diga debbo wi’ata: Mi numayno dow ɗum aran, koo: ɗum ɗon caɗtu ngam am. Irin nden goonga on woni ko nazarin ɗon ɗabbita.

intro1: These notes are designed to help group leaders and mentors get the most out of each chapter – both for the people they are leading and for themselves. They are not a substitute for working through the material yourself first. The best leaders are those who have worked through the questions personally before they help others doing the same.

Create a safe space for honesty. The questions in this study are designed to prompt real reflection, not just correct answers. Your job is to make the group feel safe enough to say what they actually think, not what they think a Christian is supposed to say.
Don’t rush the silences. Some of the best moments in a group study happen after a question lands and nobody speaks for a few seconds. Resist the urge to fill every silence.
Watch for the person who goes quiet. Sometimes the most significant thing happening in the room is with the person who says the least. Check in with them afterwards.
intro2: Wadannan bayanan an tsara su ne don taimaka wa shugabannin rukuni da masu jagoranci su sami fa’ida sosai daga kowane babi – ga mutanen da suke jagora da kuma kansu. Ba madadin nazarin kayan aikin da kanka ba ne da farko. Mafi kyawun shugabanni su ne waɗanda suka yi nazarin tambayoyin da kansu kafin su taimaka wa wasu yin hakan.

Ka samar da yanayi mai aminci don gaskiya. Tambayoyin da ke cikin wannan nazarin an tsara su ne don motsa tunani na kwarai, ba kawai amsoshi daidai ba. Aikinka shi ne ka sa rukunin su ji suna cikin aminci da za su faɗi abin da suke tunani na gaske, ba abin da suke tsammanin ya kamata Kirista ya faɗa ba.

Kada ka hanzarta lokacin shiru. Wasu daga cikin mafi kyawun lokuta a nazarin rukuni suna faruwa bayan an gabatar da tambaya kuma babu wanda ya yi magana na ƴan daƙiƙoƙi. Ka guji sha’awar cike kowane shiru.
Ka kula da mutumin da ya yi shiru. Wani lokaci abu mafi mahimmanci da ke faruwa a dakin shi ne tare da mutumin da ya fi kowa ƙarancin magana. Ka duba su bayan an gama.
intro3: Ɗee kuuje binndaɗe njiɗi nafaago ardiiɓe e ekkitinooɓe heɓugo nafa haa kooɗume babi – ngam yimɓe ɓe ɓe ardoto e ngam ko’e maɓɓe kadi. Ɗum wonaa mbadal waɗugo kuugal ngal a anndi kanko on aran. Ardiiɓe ɓuri mawnugo ngoni ɓeen waɗuɓe ƴameeje ɗee dow ko’e maɓɓe bako ɓe mballa woɓɓe waɗugo bana non.

Tiggan nokkuure hoolare ngam goonga. Ƴameeje nder janngirde nde njiɗi ummugo miijitago goonga, naa tan jaabaawol goongawol. Kuugal ma on waɗugo yimɓe nder kawtal ngal nannira hoore maɓɓe nder hoolare haa ɓe wolwa ko ɓe numata goonga, naa ko ɓe numata nassi mo Kirista’en poti wolwugo.
Taa henyu de’irre. Wakkatiiji ɓurɗi mawnugo nder janngirde kawtal ɗon laata ɓaawo ƴameende fukkaama de’irre laati ɗon haa seɗɗa. Taa nannu mbeebake cukkugo kooɗume de’irre.
Ndarna gite ma dow neɗɗo de’uɗo. Wakkati feere, huunde ɓurnde mawnugo ɗon laata nder suudu ndun woni bee neɗɗo pamaro wolde on. Ndara ɓe ɓaawo ɗon.

chapters.keyPoints1:
This opening chapter establishes the framework for the whole study: the Christian life begins and continues with repentance and faith. Neither can be left behind.

The ‘Growth Graph’ concept is central. Use it to get the group to locate themselves honestly — are they on a Graph 3 trajectory (uneven but growing) or a Graph 4 (stalled)? This is not a trick question; the honest answer is the useful one.

Draw out the link between sin and darkness (1 John 1:5-7): the things we keep hidden tend to grow. Repentance is not just a one-off act at conversion — it is the ongoing posture of the Christian life.
chapters.pastorals1:
Some people in the group may feel stuck and ashamed about it. The chapter’s tone is not condemnatory — it is hopeful. Graph 4 can become Graph 5. Make sure that message lands clearly.

Others may be tempted to tick ‘Graph 3’ automatically without real reflection. Encourage honest self-assessment; the growth graph exercise only works if people engage with it genuinely.

Watch for people who are embarrassed about ongoing sin (1 John 1:8-9). The chapter addresses this directly: pretending we have no struggles breeds hypocrisy and stalls growth. Help the group see that acknowledging sin to God — and to safe others — is the path forward, not a sign of failure.
chapters.watch1: 👁️ Watch for people who equate spiritual maturity with having fewer struggles. The chapter argues the opposite: mature Christians are more honest about their struggles, not less.

chapters.keyPoints1:
This chapter explores what it means to grow in Jesus: in obedience, in grace, and in knowledge of Christ. These three are not alternatives — they belong together.

The septic tank illustration is deliberately provocative. Use it to press the question: do we actually want to be saved from sin, or just from its consequences? Salvation and discipleship must go together (Matthew 28:19-20).

The section on grace and godliness (Titus 2:12; Romans 5:6-8, 10) is theologically important. Christ didn’t save us because we learned to obey Him. We obey because He has already saved us. This ordering matters enormously for how people approach the Christian life.
chapters.pastorals1:
Paul’s story at the start will resonate with many in the group — the cooling-off pattern is very common. Normalise it, but don’t leave it there: the point is that it doesn’t have to stay that way.

Some may struggle with the idea that God’s love doesn’t depend on their performance. This is deeply counter-intuitive for many people. Take time with Romans 5:6-8 — Christ died for us while we were still sinners, not once we had sorted ourselves out.

The Heart Check Likert scale at the end is designed to surface honest self-awareness. Encourage people to answer it privately and truthfully before any group discussion.
chapters.watch1: 👁️ Watch for people who hear ‘grace’ and conclude that obedience doesn’t matter. The chapter is clear that grace produces godliness (Titus 2:12), not a licence to coast.

chapters.keyPoints1:
This chapter presses the distinction between ageing and maturing. Time passes automatically; growth does not. Some people have been Christians for decades and are still stuck at an early stage of maturity — and that is not what God intends.

The passages from Colossians, Psalm 1, and Mark 4 together build a picture of what roots look like in practice: staying rooted in Christ (Col 2:6-7), meditating on God’s Word (Ps 1:2-3), and not allowing the thorns of life to choke growth (Mark 4:7).

The Gospel-Growth Plan section is the practical centrepiece of the chapter. It asks people to identify one specific, personal growth area — not a vague aspiration but a concrete target. This is worth significant discussion time.
chapters.pastorals1:
People may find it uncomfortable to be asked to name a specific growth area. Some will default to something safe and general. Gently push for something real — the exercise only has value if it’s honest.

Be sensitive to the fact that some growth areas (addiction, secret sin, relationship breakdown) may be deeply painful to acknowledge, even privately. The app format gives people privacy; use that as a feature, not a limitation.

The amazing destiny passage (Philippians 3:20-21) is meant to be genuinely motivating. Don’t rush past it — growth is not mere duty; it is moving towards something glorious.
chapters.watch1: 👁️ Watch for people who choose a Gospel-growth area that is really about someone else (‘my marriage’, ‘my family’). The plan should be personal and individual.

chapters.keyPoints1:
This chapter introduces sin as a predator. John Owen’s quote — ‘Be killing sin or it will be killing you’ — frames everything that follows. Sin is not a minor inconvenience; it is actively destructive.

The study of Genesis 3 repays careful attention. The serpent’s tactics — questioning God’s word, distorting His commands, promising what He hasn’t promised — are still in use today. Help the group see how these tactics work in their own experience of temptation.

The section on sin spreading (Genesis 4) shows how quickly sin escalates: from one act of disobedience to fratricide within a generation. The weed-seed illustration makes the point well — small sins are not harmless; they grow.
chapters.pastorals1:
Thomas’s story at the start is deliberately confronting. Some in the group may have their own version of it. The aim is not to shock, but to be honest that sin’s effects can be devastating — and that there is a way back.

The question ‘In your life, who is doing better at killing who?’ is intentionally direct. Give people space to sit with that question before moving on. Silence here is often productive.

Watch for people who minimise their sin by comparing it to others’. The half-truth dynamic (Genesis 3) is worth pressing: the most dangerous lies have some truth mixed in.
chapters.watch1: 👁️ Watch for people who have carried hidden sin for a long time and may feel that their situation is beyond hope. The hints of grace in Genesis 3:15 and 3:21 are there for a reason — God has not given up on His purposes of blessing.

chapters.keyPoints1:
This chapter explores sin as refusal — refusal of God’s knowledge, His authority, His goodness, and ultimately of God Himself. Reinhold Niebuhr’s opening quote frames sin as the refusal to accept our creaturely limits.

The sequence of passages builds a cumulative picture of what sin refuses: knowledge (Romans 1:18-23), God’s authority (Romans 5:14; Ezekiel 2:3-5), gratitude (Romans 1:21b), relationship (Leviticus 26:40), and finally God Himself (Romans 1:25).

The contrast with Mark 12:30 is important: the opposite of refusing God is loving Him with everything. Use this to move the discussion from diagnosis to aspiration.

The closing bridge text on sin hierarchies is worth dwelling on — Christians often tolerate invisible sins (pride, greed, envy) while policing visible ones. Jesus didn’t operate that way.
chapters.pastorals1:
This chapter is theologically dense. Don’t feel you need to cover every passage in a group setting — pick the two or three that are most relevant to where your group is.

The question ‘Which of these types of refusals are you most at risk of?’ is very personal. It may produce either very honest answers or very guarded ones. Either response is revealing.

The Pharisee dynamic in the closing bridge (Mark 2:15-17) is important pastorally: some group members may be very good at external religion while harbouring deep interior refusals. Handle with care.
chapters.watch1: 👁️ Watch for people who identify easily with the ‘refusal of authority’ examples but are blind to their own refusal of gratitude or relationship with God. Both are forms of the same thing.

chapters.keyPoints1:
This chapter turns from the diagnosis of sin to the Father’s love. The parable of the lost son (Luke 15:11-32) is the primary text, explored through the lens of its three main characters.

The younger son represents the person who has walked away from God openly. His journey — from demanding his inheritance to feeding pigs to ‘coming to his senses’ — maps onto many people’s experience of sin and its consequences.

The father’s response is the theological heart of the chapter: he sees his son ‘while he was still a long way off’, runs to him, and restores him fully. This is not a grudging acceptance — it is extravagant welcome. The robe, the ring, the fattened calf: full restoration, not probation.

The bipolar Likert scale at the start — measuring how the group perceives God’s attitude towards them — sets up a before/after comparison with chapter 7. It’s worth completing carefully.
chapters.pastorals1:
Some in the group will identify painfully with the younger son. Be ready for that. The chapter’s message is one of hope: the father hasn’t given up, and neither has God.

The distinction between leaving through the ‘front door’ and leaving through the ‘back door’ is pastorally significant. Some people in church settings have never actually come home to God — they’ve just stayed in the vicinity. Handle this gently but honestly.

The question ‘Do you mostly feel at home with God, or more distant?’ is a key pastoral barometer. Don’t rush past it.
chapters.watch1: 👁️ Watch for people who have a deeply negative view of God — as distant, judgmental, or indifferent. The parable is specifically designed to correct that picture. Let it do its work.

chapters.keyPoints1:
This chapter completes the parable by focusing on the older son and the listening audience. Jesus originally told the parable to the Pharisees — people who were religious but distant from God in a different way.

The older son’s problem is not irreligion but a transactional view of his relationship with the father. He thinks he has earned love through obedience. He is miles from his father emotionally even while living in his house. This is a searching challenge for churchgoing people.

The ‘tower block’ illustration challenges the common assumption that salvation is about being good enough. Titus 3:5 and Galatians 2:16 make clear that no one is justified by works — God cuts the building vertically, not horizontally.

The repeated bipolar Likert scale at the end allows people to compare their perception of God’s attitude towards them before and after engaging with the parable. This can be a powerful moment of reflection.
chapters.pastorals1:
The older son dynamic is often harder to surface in a group than the younger son dynamic. People who identify with the older son are often high-functioning, respectable, and resistant to the idea that they might be ‘outside the party’.

The questions ‘Am I more like the younger son or the older one?’ and ‘Am I inside the party or outside?’ are the most important in the chapter. Give them real time and real space.

The closing comparison of the two Likert scales (from chapters 6 and 7) is an opportunity for a genuinely moving moment — if people have engaged honestly, they may find that the parable has shifted something in how they see God. Be attentive to that.
chapters.watch1: 👁️ Watch for people who give intellectual assent to the gospel (‘yes, I know God loves me’) without any emotional reality to it. The parable is designed to move people, not just inform them.

paras: Rick Creighton grew up in Northern Ireland and studied maths at Oxford and then taught maths at a secondary school in England. After that, he studied theology at Moore Theological College in Sydney and London School of Theology.

He served as a church pastor in England for ten years, before moving to Nigeria in 2012, where he has been teaching at ECWA Theological Seminary Kagoro and Jos ECWA Theological Seminary. He has been involved with BeaconLight Training since 2003.
Rick is married to Alanna. They have three children: Asha, Conor, and Jack.

publisher.name: BeaconLight
paras: BeaconLight is a non-denominational Christian training organisation based in the UK, founded in 1995. Its aim is to equip Christians worldwide with practically-applied Biblical gospel ministry tools — resources designed to help people both equip themselves and equip others.

BeaconLight’s resources include CrossCheck, which enables Christians to explain their faith and lead others to Jesus, and Word@Work, a verse-by-verse applied devotional commentary of the whole New Testament. BeaconLight also provides printed Biblical resources to prisons, seafarers, police, and the armed services.
BeaconLight believes that Christian ministry should be free of charge, and all its online materials are available without registration or payment. The ministry is funded entirely by donations.
BeaconLight is a member of the Evangelical Alliance and Global Connections, and is a registered charity in England and Wales (Charity No. 1047046).
